subject: Math
description: Partition circles and rectangles into two, three, or four equal shares, describe the shares using the words halves, thirds, half of, a third of, etc., and describe the whole as two halves, three thirds, four fourths. Recognize that equal shares of identical wholes need not have the same shape.
description_plain: Your child can split circles and rectangles into equal parts (like halves, thirds, or fourths), name these parts, and understand that equal parts can look different.

subject: Math
description: Partition a rectangle into rows and columns of same-size squares and count to find the total number of them.
description_plain: Your child can divide a rectangle into rows and columns of equal squares and count how many squares there are in total.

subject: Math
description: Recognize and draw shapes having specified attributes, such as a given number of angles or a given number of equal faces. Identify triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.
description_plain: Your child can recognize and draw shapes with certain features, like a set number of corners or sides, and can name shapes like triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.

subject: Math
description: Reason with shapes and their attributes.
description_plain: Your child understands how to think about shapes and their features.

subject: Math
description: Draw a picture graph and a bar graph (with single-unit scale) to represent a data set with up to four categories. Solve simple put-together, take-apart, and compare problems using information presented in a bar graph.
description_plain: Your child can make picture and bar graphs to show data in up to four groups and use the graphs to solve simple math problems.

subject: Math
description: Generate measurement data by measuring lengths of several objects to the nearest whole unit, or by making repeated measurements of the same object. Show the measurements by making a line plot, where the horizontal scale is marked off in whole-number units.
description_plain: Your child can measure the lengths of objects and show the results on a simple line graph using whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Your child can show and understand information using graphs and charts.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving dollar bills, quarters, dimes, nickels, and pennies, using $ and ¢ symbols appropriately.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems using coins and bills, and use the $ and ¢ symbols correctly.

subject: Math
description: Tell and write time from analog and digital clocks to the nearest five minutes, using a.m. and p.m.
description_plain: Your child can tell and write the time to the nearest five minutes on both analog and digital clocks, using a.m. and p.m.

subject: Math
description: Work with time and money.
description_plain: Your child can work with time and money in everyday situations.

subject: Math
description: Represent whole numbers as lengths from 0 on a number line diagram with equally spaced points corresponding to the numbers 0, 1, 2, …, and represent whole-number sums and differences within 100 on a number line diagram.
description_plain: Your child can show numbers as distances on a number line and use the number line to add or subtract numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve word problems involving lengths that are given in the same units, e.g., by using drawings (such as drawings of rulers) and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about length by adding or subtracting measurements using drawings or equations.

subject: Math
description: Relate addition and subtraction to length.
description_plain: Your child can connect addition and subtraction to measuring length.

subject: Math
description: Measure to determine how much longer one object is than another, expressing the length difference in terms of a standard length unit.
description_plain: Your child can measure two objects and tell how much longer one is than the other using standard units like inches or centimeters.

subject: Math
description: Estimate lengths using units of inches, feet, centimeters, and meters.
description_plain: Your child can make good guesses about how long things are using inches, feet, centimeters, or meters.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object twice, using length units of different lengths for the two measurements; describe how the two measurements relate to the size of the unit chosen.
description_plain: Your child can measure the same object with different tools and explain how the size of the unit changes the measurement.

subject: Math
description: Measure the length of an object by selecting and using appropriate tools such as rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, and measuring tapes.
description_plain: Your child can choose the right tool and measure how long something is using rulers, yardsticks, meter sticks, or measuring tapes.

subject: Math
description: Measure and estimate lengths in standard units.
description_plain: Your child can measure and estimate lengths using standard units.

subject: Math
description: Explain why addition and subtraction strategies work, using place value and the properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can explain why their ways of adding and subtracting work by using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Mentally add 10 or 100 to a given number 100—900, and mentally subtract 10 or 100 from a given number 100—900.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add or subtract 10 or 100 to numbers between 100 and 900 in their head.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 1000, using concrete models or drawings and strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction; relate the strategy to a written method. Understand that in adding or subtracting three-digit numbers, one adds or subtracts hundreds and hundreds, tens and tens, ones and ones; and sometimes it is necessary to compose or decompose tens or hundreds.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using objects, drawings, or math strategies, and understands how to add or subtract hundreds, tens, and ones.

subject: Math
description: Add up to four two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
description_plain: Your child can add up to four two-digit numbers using place value and math rules.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 100 using strategies based on place value, properties of operations, and/or the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add and subtract numbers up to 100 using different math strategies.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to add and subtract.
description_plain: Your child can use their understanding of place value and math rules to add and subtract numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two three-digit numbers based on meanings of the hundreds, tens, and ones digits, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can compare two three-digit numbers and use the symbols >, =, and < to show which is bigger, smaller, or equal.

subject: Math
description: Read and write numbers to 1000 using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form.
description_plain: Your child can read and write numbers up to 1000 in different ways, like with digits, words, or expanded form.

subject: Math
description: Count within 1000; skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.
description_plain: Your child can count up to 1000 and skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.

subject: Math
description: The numbers 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900 refer to one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine hundreds (and 0 tens and 0 ones).
description_plain: Your child understands that numbers like 100, 200, or 900 mean one hundred, two hundreds, or nine hundreds with no extra tens or ones.

subject: Math
description: 100 can be thought of as a bundle of ten tens — called a "hundred."
description_plain: Your child understands that 100 is the same as ten groups of ten.

subject: Math
description: Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones; e.g., 706 equals 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones. Understand the following as special cases:
description_plain: Your child understands that in a three-digit number, each digit shows how many hundreds, tens, or ones there are.

subject: Math
description: Understand place value.
description_plain: Your child understands how place value works for numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns; write an equation to express the total as a sum of equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can use addition to find the total number of objects in a rectangle with up to 5 rows and 5 columns, and write an equation to show this.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20) has an odd or even number of members, e.g., by pairing objects or counting them by 2s; write an equation to express an even number as a sum of two equal addends.
description_plain: Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects is odd or even, and can write an equation to show an even number as two equal parts.

subject: Math
description: Work with equal groups of objects to gain foundations for multiplication.
description_plain: Your child can work with equal groups of objects to get ready for learning multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract within 20 using mental strategies. By end of Grade 2, know from memory all sums of two one-digit numbers.
description_plain: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 20 in their head and knows all the sums of two one-digit numbers from memory.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract within 20.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 20 easily.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one- and two-step word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can use addition and subtraction to solve one- and two-step word problems with numbers up to 100, using drawings or equations to show their thinking.

subject: Math
description: Represent and solve problems involving addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can solve math problems using addition and subtraction.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child can notice patterns in math and use them to solve problems more easily.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child can find and use helpful structures or patterns in math problems.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Your child can use math words and numbers carefully and accurately.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child can choose and use the right tools, like rulers or calculators, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Your child can use math to solve real-life problems and show their thinking with pictures, numbers, or objects.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and discuss others' ideas about math.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about numbers and problems in different ways, using both words and numbers to explain.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child can understand what a math problem is asking and keep working until they solve it, even if it's hard.

subject: Math
description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two decimals refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with the symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual model.
description_plain: Your child can compare two decimals to the hundredths place, use >, =, or < symbols, and explain their thinking using pictures if needed.

subject: Math
description: Use decimal notation for fractions with denominators 10 or 100.
description_plain: Your child can write fractions with denominators of 10 or 100 as decimals.

subject: Math
description: Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100, and use this technique to add two fractions with respective denominators 10 and 100.
description_plain: Your child can change a fraction with 10 on the bottom to an equivalent fraction with 100 on the bottom, and use this to add fractions like 3/10 + 4/100.

subject: Math
description: Understand decimal notation for fractions, and compare decimal fractions.
description_plain: Your child understands how to write fractions as decimals and can compare decimal fractions.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving multiplication of a fraction by a whole number, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems where they need to multiply a fraction by a whole number, using pictures or equations to help.

subject: Math
description: Understand a multiple of a/b as a multiple of 1/b, and use this understanding to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child understands that multiplying a fraction by a whole number is like adding the fraction to itself several times, and can do this multiplication.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction like 3/4 means three pieces of size 1/4.

subject: Math
description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction by a whole number.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about multiplication to multiply a fraction by a whole number.

subject: Math
description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole and having like denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models and equations to represent the problem.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that involve adding or subtracting fractions with the same bottom number, using pictures or equations to show their work.

subject: Math
description: Add and subtract mixed numbers with like denominators, e.g., by replacing each mixed number with an equivalent fraction, and/or by using properties of operations and the relationship between addition and subtraction.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract mixed numbers with the same bottom number, using different methods like changing them to fractions or using math rules.

subject: Math
description: Decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way, recording each decomposition by an equation. Justify decompositions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can break a fraction into a sum of smaller fractions with the same bottom number in different ways, and show or explain how they did it.

subject: Math
description: Understand addition and subtraction of fractions as joining and separating parts referring to the same whole.
description_plain: Your child understands that adding and subtracting fractions is like putting together or taking apart parts of the same whole.

subject: Math
description: Understand a fraction a/b with a > 1 as a sum of fractions 1/b.
description_plain: Your child understands that a fraction like 5/6 means five pieces of size 1/6 added together.

subject: Math
description: Build fractions from unit fractions by applying and extending previous understandings of operations on whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child can build and work with fractions by using what they know about adding, subtracting, and multiplying whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators, e.g., by creating common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to a benchmark fraction such as 1/2. Recognize that comparisons are valid only when the two fractions refer to the same whole. Record the results of comparisons with symbols >, =, or <, and justify the conclusions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model.
description_plain: Your child can compare two fractions with different top and bottom numbers by making common denominators or using benchmarks like 1/2, use >, =, or < symbols, and explain their thinking with pictures if needed.

subject: Math
description: Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n × a)/(n × b) by using visual fraction models, with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the two fractions themselves are the same size. Use this principle to recognize and generate equivalent fractions.
description_plain: Your child can explain why two fractions are equal, even if they look different, by using pictures and making equivalent fractions.

subject: Math
description: Extend understanding of fraction equivalence and ordering.
description_plain: Your child understands how to tell when fractions are equal and how to put them in order.

subject: Math
description: Recognize a line of symmetry for a two-dimensional figure as a line across the figure such that the figure can be folded along the line into matching parts. Identify line-symmetric figures and draw lines of symmetry.
description_plain: Your child can find and draw lines of symmetry in shapes, and tell which shapes have matching halves.

subject: Math
description: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles of a specified size. Recognize right triangles as a category, and identify right triangles.
description_plain: Your child can sort shapes by looking for parallel or perpendicular lines or certain angles, and can recognize and name right triangles.

subject: Math
description: Draw points, lines, line segments, rays, angles (right, acute, obtuse), and perpendicular and parallel lines. Identify these in two-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can draw and identify points, lines, line segments, rays, and different types of angles, and find these in shapes.

subject: Math
description: Draw and identify lines and angles, and classify shapes by properties of their lines and angles.
description_plain: Your child can draw and recognize lines and angles, and sort shapes based on their lines and angles.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angle measure as additive. When an angle is decomposed into non-overlapping parts, the angle measure of the whole is the sum of the angle measures of the parts. Solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram in real world and mathematical problems, e.g., by using an equation with a symbol for the unknown angle measure.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles can be split into parts, and can solve problems to find missing angles by adding or subtracting.

subject: Math
description: Measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor. Sketch angles of specified measure.
description_plain: Your child can measure angles in whole degrees with a protractor and draw angles of a given size.

subject: Math
description: An angle that turns through n one-degree angles is said to have an angle measure of n degrees.
description_plain: Your child understands that if an angle turns through n one-degree steps, it measures n degrees.

subject: Math
description: An angle is measured with reference to a circle with its center at the common endpoint of the rays, by considering the fraction of the circular arc between the points where the two rays intersect the circle. An angle that turns through 1/360 of a circle is called a "one-degree angle," and can be used to measure angles.
description_plain: Your child understands that angles are measured by how much they turn around a point, and that a one-degree angle is 1/360 of a full circle.

subject: Math
description: Recognize angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint, and understand concepts of angle measurement:
description_plain: Your child knows that angles are made when two rays meet at a point, and understands how to measure angles.

subject: Math
description: Geometric measurement: understand concepts of angle and measure angles.
description_plain: Your child understands what angles are and how to measure them.

subject: Math
description: Make a line plot to display a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit (1/2, 1/4, 1/8). Solve problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions by using information presented in line plots.
description_plain: Your child can make a line plot to show measurements in fractions and use the plot to solve addition and subtraction problems with fractions.

subject: Math
description: Represent and interpret data.
description_plain: Your child can show and understand data using charts or plots.

subject: Math
description: Apply the area and perimeter formulas for rectangles in real world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the area and perimeter of rectangles in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations to solve word problems involving distances, intervals of time, liquid volumes, masses of objects, and money, including problems involving simple fractions or decimals, and problems that require expressing measurements given in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Represent measurement quantities using diagrams such as number line diagrams that feature a measurement scale.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems about distance, time, liquid, weight, and money, including those with fractions or decimals, and can show measurements on diagrams like number lines.

subject: Math
description: Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system of units including km, m, cm; kg, g; lb, oz.; l, ml; hr, min, sec. Within a single system of measurement, express measurements in a larger unit in terms of a smaller unit. Record measurement equivalents in a two column table.
description_plain: Your child knows the sizes of different measurement units (like meters, centimeters, kilograms, grams) and can change measurements from bigger units to smaller ones, recording them in a table.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving measurement and conversion of measurements from a larger unit to a smaller unit.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems that involve measuring and changing measurements from larger units to smaller units.

subject: Math
description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can divide numbers up to four digits by a one-digit number, find the answer and remainder, and show their work with equations, arrays, or area models.

subject: Math
description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number, and multiply two two-digit numbers, using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations. Illustrate and explain the calculation by using equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.
description_plain: Your child can multiply a large number (up to four digits) by a one-digit number, and multiply two two-digit numbers, and can show and explain their work using pictures, equations, or area models.

subject: Math
description: Fluently add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm.
description_plain: Your child can quickly and accurately add and subtract large numbers using the standard way taught in school.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding and properties of operations to perform multi-digit arithmetic.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use place value and math rules to solve problems with large numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use place value understanding to round multi-digit whole numbers to any place.
description_plain: Your child can round large numbers to any place value, like the nearest ten, hundred, or thousand.

subject: Math
description: Read and write multi-digit whole numbers using base-ten numerals, number names, and expanded form. Compare two multi-digit numbers based on meanings of the digits in each place, using >, =, and < symbols to record the results of comparisons.
description_plain: Your child can read and write large numbers in different ways, and can compare them using the symbols >, =, and <.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right.
description_plain: Your child understands that each place in a number is worth ten times more than the place to its right.

subject: Math
description: Generalize place value understanding for multi-digit whole numbers.
description_plain: Your child understands how place value works for numbers with more than one digit.

subject: Math
description: Generate a number or shape pattern that follows a given rule. Identify apparent features of the pattern that were not explicit in the rule itself.
description_plain: Your child can make number or shape patterns by following a rule and can notice things about the pattern that aren't directly stated in the rule.

subject: Math
description: Generate and analyze patterns.
description_plain: Your child can create and study patterns in numbers or shapes.

subject: Math
description: Find all factor pairs for a whole number in the range 1—100. Recognize that a whole number is a multiple of each of its factors. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1—100 is a multiple of a given one-digit number. Determine whether a given whole number in the range 1—100 is prime or composite.
description_plain: Your child can find all the pairs of numbers that multiply to make a number between 1 and 100, can tell if a number is a multiple of another, and can decide if a number is prime or composite.

subject: Math
description: Gain familiarity with factors and multiples.
description_plain: Your child is learning about factors (numbers that multiply together) and multiples (numbers you get by multiplying).

subject: Math
description: Solve multistep word problems posed with whole numbers and having whole-number answers using the four operations, including problems in which remainders must be interpreted. Represent these problems using equations with a letter standing for the unknown quantity. Assess the reasonableness of answers using mental computation and estimation strategies including rounding.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems that take more than one step using addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division, and can check if their answers make sense by estimating or rounding.

subject: Math
description: Multiply or divide to solve word problems involving multiplicative comparison, e.g., by using drawings and equations with a symbol for the unknown number to represent the problem, distinguishing multiplicative comparison from additive comparison.
description_plain: Your child can solve word problems where one amount is many times bigger than another by multiplying or dividing, and can show their thinking with drawings or equations.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison, e.g., interpret 35 = 5 × 7 as a statement that 35 is 5 times as many as 7 and 7 times as many as 5. Represent verbal statements of multiplicative comparisons as multiplication equations.
description_plain: Your child understands that a multiplication equation can show how many times bigger one number is than another, and can write these comparisons as equations.

subject: Math
description: Use the four operations with whole numbers to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can use addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division to solve different kinds of problems.

subject: Math
description: Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.
description_plain: Your child notices patterns in math and uses them to solve problems more easily.

subject: Math
description: Look for and make use of structure.
description_plain: Your child looks for ways to break down and organize math problems to make them easier to solve.

subject: Math
description: Attend to precision.
description_plain: Your child pays attention to details and tries to be accurate in math work and answers.

subject: Math
description: Use appropriate tools strategically.
description_plain: Your child chooses the best tools, like rulers, calculators, or drawings, to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model with mathematics.
description_plain: Your child uses math to solve real-life problems and can show how math applies to everyday situations.

subject: Math
description: Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
description_plain: Your child can explain their math thinking and listen to and discuss the ideas of others.

subject: Math
description: Reason abstractly and quantitatively.
description_plain: Your child can think about numbers and problems in different ways, using both pictures and numbers.

subject: Math
description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
description_plain: Your child keeps trying to solve math problems, even when they are hard, and looks for ways to understand them.

subject: Math
description: (+) Prove the addition and subtraction formulas for sine, cosine, and tangent and use them to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can show why the formulas for adding and subtracting sine, cosine, and tangent work, and use them to solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: (+) Prove the addition and subtraction formulas for sine, cosine, and tangent and use them to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can show why the formulas for adding and subtracting sine, cosine, and tangent work, and use them to solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: (+) Prove the addition and subtraction formulas for sine, cosine, and tangent and use them to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can show why the formulas for adding and subtracting sine, cosine, and tangent work, and use them to solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: (+) Prove the addition and subtraction formulas for sine, cosine, and tangent and use them to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can show why the formulas for adding and subtracting sine, cosine, and tangent work, and use them to solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Prove the Pythagorean identity sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 and use it to find sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) given sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) and the quadrant of the angle.
description_plain: Your child can prove that sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 and use this fact to find missing values for sine, cosine, or tangent if they know one of them and which part of the circle the angle is in.

subject: Math
description: Prove the Pythagorean identity sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 and use it to find sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) given sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) and the quadrant of the angle.
description_plain: Your child can prove that sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 and use this fact to find missing values for sine, cosine, or tangent if they know one of them and which part of the circle the angle is in.

subject: Math
description: Prove the Pythagorean identity sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 and use it to find sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) given sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) and the quadrant of the angle.
description_plain: Your child can prove that sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 and use this fact to find missing values for sine, cosine, or tangent if they know one of them and which part of the circle the angle is in.

subject: Math
description: Prove the Pythagorean identity sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 and use it to find sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) given sin(θ), cos(θ), or tan(θ) and the quadrant of the angle.
description_plain: Your child can prove that sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 and use this fact to find missing values for sine, cosine, or tangent if they know one of them and which part of the circle the angle is in.

subject: Math
description: Prove and apply trigonometric identities
description_plain: Your child can show and use important math facts about sine, cosine, and tangent to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Prove and apply trigonometric identities
description_plain: Your child can show and use important math facts about sine, cosine, and tangent to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Prove and apply trigonometric identities
description_plain: Your child can show and use important math facts about sine, cosine, and tangent to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Prove and apply trigonometric identities
description_plain: Your child can show and use important math facts about sine, cosine, and tangent to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: (+) Use inverse functions to solve trigonometric equations that arise in modeling contexts; evaluate the solutions using technology, and interpret them in terms of the context.
description_plain: Your child can use special math functions to solve equations with sine, cosine, or tangent, check their answers with technology, and explain what the answers mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: (+) Use inverse functions to solve trigonometric equations that arise in modeling contexts; evaluate the solutions using technology, and interpret them in terms of the context.
description_plain: Your child can use special math functions to solve equations with sine, cosine, or tangent, check their answers with technology, and explain what the answers mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: (+) Use inverse functions to solve trigonometric equations that arise in modeling contexts; evaluate the solutions using technology, and interpret them in terms of the context.
description_plain: Your child can use special math functions to solve equations with sine, cosine, or tangent, check their answers with technology, and explain what the answers mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: (+) Use inverse functions to solve trigonometric equations that arise in modeling contexts; evaluate the solutions using technology, and interpret them in terms of the context.
description_plain: Your child can use special math functions to solve equations with sine, cosine, or tangent, check their answers with technology, and explain what the answers mean in real situations.

subject: Math
description: (+) Understand that restricting a trigonometric function to a domain on which it is always increasing or always decreasing allows its inverse to be constructed.
description_plain: Your child understands that by looking at just part of a sine, cosine, or tangent graph where it always goes up or down, they can create a matching 'reverse' function.

subject: Math
description: (+) Understand that restricting a trigonometric function to a domain on which it is always increasing or always decreasing allows its inverse to be constructed.
description_plain: Your child understands that by focusing on certain parts of a trigonometric function, we can create its opposite (inverse) function.

description: Understand that a function from one set (called the domain) to another set (called the range) assigns to each element of the domain exactly one element of the range. If f is a function and x is an element of its domain, then f(x) denotes the output of f corresponding to the input x. The graph of f is the graph of the equation y = f(x).
description_plain: Your child understands that a function matches each input to exactly one output, and can use and read function notation and graphs.
