description: Use data presented in social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts to answer compelling and supporting questions.
description_plain: Your child can use information from charts, graphs, maps, and texts to answer questions about social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Know how to find relevant evidence from a variety of sources.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find useful information from different places, like books, websites, or pictures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use supporting questions to help answer compelling social studies questions.
description_plain: Your child can use smaller questions to help answer bigger questions about social studies topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between compelling questions and supporting questions.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between big, important questions and smaller, supporting questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use compelling and supporting questions to investigate social studies problems.
description_plain: Your child can use both big and small questions to explore and learn about social studies problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Inquiry, Research, And Analysis
description_plain: Your child will learn how to ask questions, do research, and study information in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify point of view and bias.
description_plain: Your child can spot when someone is sharing their own opinion or showing favoritism.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express social science ideas or information in written, spoken, and graphic forms including tables, line graphs, bar graphs, and maps.
description_plain: Your child can share ideas or information by writing, speaking, or making charts, graphs, and maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between primary and secondary source documents.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between information from people who saw something happen and information from people who learned about it later.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use appropriate strategies to read and interpret basic social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts.
description_plain: Your child can use different ways, like reading or looking at charts and maps, to understand social studies information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Reading And Communication – Read And Communicate Effectively
description_plain: Your child will learn how to read and share information clearly in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in projects to help or inform others.
description_plain: Your child can join in projects that help or teach others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop and implement an action plan and know how, when, and where to address or inform others about a public issue.
description_plain: Your child can make a plan to help with a community problem and knows how and when to share information about it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act constructively to further the public good.
description_plain: Your child will take positive actions to help their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compose a paragraph expressing a position on a public policy issue in Michigan and justify the position with a reasoned argument.
description_plain: Your child can write a paragraph about a Michigan community issue and explain their opinion with reasons.

subject: Social Studies
description: Communicate a reasoned position on a public issue.
description_plain: Your child can clearly share their opinion about a community problem and explain why they feel that way.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how conflicts over Democratic Values lead people to differ on resolutions to a public policy issue in Michigan.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how people disagree about Michigan community issues because of different beliefs about fairness and rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use graphic data and other sources to analyze information about a public issue in Michigan and evaluate alternative resolutions.
description_plain: Your child can use charts and other information to study a Michigan problem and think about different solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify public issues in Michigan that influence the daily lives of its citizens.
description_plain: Your child can name problems in Michigan that affect people's daily lives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Clearly state a problem as a public policy issue, analyze various perspectives, and generate and evaluate possible alternative resolutions.
description_plain: Your child can clearly describe a community problem, look at different opinions, and think about possible solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify products produced in other countries and consumed by people in Michigan.
description_plain: Your child can name things made in other countries that people in Michigan use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use fundamental principles and concepts of economics to understand economic activity in the global economy.
description_plain: Your child will learn basic ideas about how the world’s economy works and how countries trade and do business.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using a Michigan example, explain how specialization leads to increased interdependence. Use fundamental principles and concepts of economics to understand economic activity in the global economy.
description_plain: Your child can explain, using a Michigan example, how people or places that focus on one job or product need to work with others to get what they need.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use fundamental principles and concepts of economics to understand economic activity in the United States.
description_plain: Your child will learn basic ideas about how the United States economy works.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of entrepreneurship and business development in Michigan's economic future.
description_plain: Your child can explain how new businesses and people who start them can help Michigan’s economy in the future.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how entrepreneurs combine natural, human, and capital resources to produce goods and services in Michigan.
description_plain: Your child can describe how business owners in Michigan use natural resources, people, and tools to make products or services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how Michigan's location and natural resources influenced its economic development.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Michigan’s location and natural resources have helped its economy grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify incentives that influence economic decisions people make in Michigan.
description_plain: Your child can identify reasons why people in Michigan make certain economic choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using a Michigan example, explain how scarcity, choice, and opportunity cost affect what is produced and consumed.
description_plain: Your child can use a Michigan example to explain how having limited resources means people must make choices and give up other options.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use fundamental principles and concepts of economics to understand economic activity in a market economy.
description_plain: Your child can understand basic ideas about how buying and selling work in our economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain rights and responsibilities of citizenship.
description_plain: Your child can name and explain the rights and responsibilities that come with being a citizen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain important rights and how, when, and where members of American society demonstrate their responsibilities by actively participating in civic life.
description_plain: Your child can explain important rights and give examples of how people show responsibility by taking part in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the purpose of the Michigan Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can describe why Michigan has its own constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how state courts function to resolve conflict.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Michigan's state courts help solve disagreements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the three branches of state government in Michigan and the powers of each.
description_plain: Your child can name Michigan's three branches of government and tell what each one does.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify goods and services provided by the state government and describe how they are funded.
description_plain: Your child can list services the state government provides and explain how they are paid for.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between the roles of tribal, state, and local governments.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between what tribal, state, and local governments do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the structure of government in the United States and how it functions.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the United States government is set up and how it works.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the Michigan state government reflects the principle of representative government.
description_plain: Your child can explain how Michigan's government lets people choose leaders to represent them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give an example of how Michigan state government fulfills one of the purposes of government.
description_plain: Your child can give an example of how Michigan's government helps its people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why people create governments.
description_plain: Your child can explain why people create governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate natural resources in Michigan and explain the consequences of their use.
description_plain: Your child can find natural resources in Michigan and explain what happens when people use them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how people are a part of, adapt to, use, and modify the physical environment of Michigan.
description_plain: Your child can describe how people in Michigan live with, use, and change their environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the effects of human-environment interactions.
description_plain: Your child understands how people and the environment affect each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use data and current information about the Anishinaabek and other Indigenous Peoples living in Michigan today to describe the cultural aspects of modern life.
description_plain: Your child can use information about the Anishinaabek and other Indigenous Peoples in Michigan today to describe their cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe some of the current movements of goods, people, jobs, or information to, from, or within Michigan and explain reasons for the movements.
description_plain: Your child can describe how and why goods, people, jobs, or information move to, from, or within Michigan.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe diverse groups that have migrated into a region of Michigan and reasons why they came (push/pull factors).
description_plain: Your child can describe different groups who moved to Michigan and explain why they came.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe major kinds of economic activity in Michigan today, such as agriculture, forestry, manufacturing, services and tourism, and research and development, and explain the factors influencing the location of these economic activities.
description_plain: Your child can name major types of work in Michigan and explain why these jobs are found in certain places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand how human activities help shape the Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child understands how what people do changes the land and environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe different regions to which Michigan belongs.
description_plain: Your child can describe the different regions that Michigan is part of.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of visual materials and data sources to describe ways in which Michigan can be divided into regions.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and pictures to show how Michigan can be divided into different regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand how regions are created from common physical and human characteristics.
description_plain: Your child understands how areas are grouped into regions based on things they have in common.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a world map to describe North America in relation to the equator and other continents and oceans, and Michigan within North America.
description_plain: Your child can use a world map to show where North America is compared to the equator, other continents, and oceans, and where Michigan is in North America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use thematic maps to identify and describe the physical and human characteristics of Michigan.
description_plain: Your child can use special maps to describe what Michigan looks like and who lives there.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use cardinal directions (north, south, east, west) to describe the relative locations of significant places in the immediate environment.
description_plain: Your child can use north, south, east, and west to describe where important places are nearby.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic representations to acquire, process, and report information from a spatial perspective.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and other tools to find and share information about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create a timeline to sequence and describe major eras and events in early Michigan history.
description_plain: Your child can make a timeline to show and describe important times and events in early Michigan history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how Michigan attained statehood.
description_plain: Your child can describe how Michigan became a state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use case studies or stories to describe how the ideas or actions of individuals affected the history of Michigan (pre-statehood).
description_plain: Your child can use stories or examples to show how people's actions changed Michigan's history before it became a state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of primary and secondary sources to construct a historical narrative about daily life in the early settlements of Michigan (pre-statehood).
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to tell what daily life was like in early Michigan settlements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of sources to describe interactions that occurred between Indigenous Peoples and the first European explorers and settlers in Michigan.
description_plain: Your child can use information to describe how Indigenous Peoples and the first European settlers in Michigan interacted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use informational text and visual data to compare how Indigenous Peoples and non-Indigenous Peoples in the early history of Michigan interacted with, adapted to, used, and/or modified their environments.
description_plain: Your child can compare how Indigenous and non-Indigenous Peoples in early Michigan used and changed their environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Draw upon traditional stories and/or teachings of Indigenous Peoples who lived and continue to live in Michigan in order to better understand their beliefs and histories.
description_plain: Your child can use traditional stories from Indigenous Peoples in Michigan to better understand their beliefs and history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causal relationships between three events in Michigan’s past.
description_plain: Your child can explain how three events in Michigan's past are connected.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historians use primary and secondary sources to answer questions about the past.
description_plain: Your child can explain how historians use different sources to answer questions about the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify questions historians ask in examining the past in Michigan.
description_plain: Your child can name questions historians ask when learning about Michigan's past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use historical thinking to understand the past.
description_plain: Your child can use different ways of thinking to understand what happened in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use democratic procedures to make decisions on civic issues in the school or classroom.
description_plain: Your child can use voting or other fair methods to help make decisions about issues in class or at school.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different strategies students and others could take to address problems and predict possible results.
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways to solve problems and guess what might happen with each choice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess options for individuals and groups to plan and conduct activities intended to advance views on matters of public policy.
description_plain: Your child can think about different ways people can work together to share their opinions and make changes about community rules or laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act out of the rule of law and hold others to the same standard.
description_plain: Your child will follow the rules and expect others to do the same.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civic Participation
description_plain: Your child understands how to take part in their community and help make decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the challenges people have faced and actions they have taken to address issues at different times and places.
description_plain: Your child can talk about problems people have faced in history and what they did to solve them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct an argument and justify a decision supported with evidence.
description_plain: Your child can make a choice about an issue and explain why, using facts to support their answer.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply Democratic Values or Constitutional Principles to support a position on an issue.
description_plain: Your child can use ideas like fairness and freedom to explain their opinion about a problem.

subject: Social Studies
description: State an issue as a question of public policy and discuss possible solutions from different perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can ask a question about a community rule or law and talk about different ways to solve the problem.

subject: Social Studies
description: Public Discourse And Decision Making
description_plain: Your child understands how to talk about problems in the community and help make decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use data presented in social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts to answer compelling and supporting questions.
description_plain: Your child can use information from charts, graphs, maps, and texts to answer questions about social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Know how to find relevant evidence from a variety of sources.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find useful information from different places to help answer questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use supporting questions to help answer compelling social studies questions.
description_plain: Your child can ask smaller questions to help answer bigger social studies questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between compelling questions and supporting questions.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between main questions and smaller, supporting questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use compelling and supporting questions to investigate social studies problems.
description_plain: Your child can use main and supporting questions to explore and learn about social studies topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Inquiry, Research, And Analysis
description_plain: Your child understands how to ask questions, do research, and study information in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify point of view and bias.
description_plain: Your child can recognize when someone is showing their own opinion or being unfair in what they say or write.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express social science ideas or information in written, spoken, and graphic forms including tables, line graphs, bar graphs, and maps.
description_plain: Your child can share ideas and information by writing, speaking, or using charts, graphs, and maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between primary and secondary source documents.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between original documents and those written later about the same topic.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use appropriate strategies to read and interpret basic social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts.
description_plain: Your child can read and understand simple charts, graphs, maps, and texts about social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Reading And Communication – Read And Communicate Effectively
description_plain: Your child can read and share information clearly in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in projects to help or inform others.
description_plain: Your child can take part in projects that help or teach others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop and implement an action plan and know how, when, and where to address or inform others about a public issue.
description_plain: Your child can make a plan to solve a community problem and knows how and when to share it with others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act constructively to further the public good.
description_plain: Your child can take positive actions to help their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compose a brief essay expressing a position on a public policy issue in the United States and justify the position with a reasoned argument.
description_plain: Your child can write a short essay about a community rule or law and explain their opinion with good reasons.

subject: Social Studies
description: Communicate a reasoned position on a public issue.
description_plain: Your child can clearly explain their opinion about a community problem.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how conflicts over Democratic Values lead people to differ on resolutions to a public policy issue in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how people disagree about fairness and freedom when solving community problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use graphic data and other sources to analyze information about a public issue in the United States and evaluate alternative resolutions.
description_plain: Your child can use charts and other information to study a community problem and think about different solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify public issues in the United States that influence the daily lives of its citizens.
description_plain: Your child can name problems in the United States that affect people's everyday lives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Clearly state a problem as a public policy issue, analyze various perspectives, and generate and evaluate possible alternative resolutions.
description_plain: Your child can clearly describe a community problem, look at different opinions, and think about possible solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify advantages and disadvantages of global competition.
description_plain: Your child can list good and bad things about countries competing with each other in business.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use fundamental principles and concepts of economics to understand economic activity in the global economy.
description_plain: Your child understands basic ideas about how the world economy works.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how changes in the United States economy impact levels of employment and unemployment.
description_plain: Your child can explain how changes in the U.S. economy affect jobs and unemployment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use fundamental principles and concepts of economics to understand economic activity in the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands basic ideas about how the U.S. economy works.

subject: Social Studies
description: List goods and services governments provide in a market economy and explain how these goods and services are funded.
description_plain: Your child can name goods and services the government provides and explain how they are paid for.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of money in the exchange of goods and services.
description_plain: Your child can explain how money is used to buy and sell things.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how competition among buyers results in higher prices, and competition among sellers results in lower prices.
description_plain: Your child can explain that when buyers compete, prices go up, and when sellers compete, prices go down.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how specialization and division of labor increase productivity.
description_plain: Your child can explain how dividing up work and letting people specialize helps everyone get more done.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how price affects decisions about purchasing goods and services.
description_plain: Your child can explain how prices affect what people choose to buy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how positive and negative incentives influence behavior in a market economy.
description_plain: Your child can describe how rewards or penalties can change people's choices in the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe characteristics of a market economy.
description_plain: Your child can describe what makes a market economy different from other types of economies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify a good or service produced in the United States and apply the three economic questions all economies must address.
description_plain: Your child can name a product or service made in the U.S. and answer the three basic economic questions: What to produce? How to produce it? Who gets it?

subject: Social Studies
description: Use fundamental principles and concepts of economics to understand economic activity in a market economy.
description_plain: Your child can use basic ideas about money, buying, and selling to understand how our economy works.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe ways in which people can work together to promote the values and principles of American democracy.
description_plain: Your child can describe ways people work together to support American values like fairness and freedom.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain rights of citizenship, why rights have limits, and the relationships between rights and responsibilities.
description_plain: Your child understands what rights citizens have, why there are limits to those rights, and how rights come with responsibilities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the responsibilities of members of American society.
description_plain: Your child can explain what responsibilities people have as members of American society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain important rights and how, when, and where members of American society demonstrate their responsibilities by actively participating in civic life.
description_plain: Your child can explain important rights and give examples of how people show responsibility by taking part in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the federal government uses taxes and spending to serve the purposes of government.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the federal government collects taxes and spends money to help the country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the President, members of the Congress, Supreme Court Justices are elected or appointed.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the President, Congress members, and Supreme Court Justices are chosen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how the system of checks and balances limits the power of the federal government.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how the government’s branches keep each other from having too much power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the powers of the federal government are separated among the branches.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the government’s power is divided among different branches.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the organizational structure of the federal government in the United States (legislative, executive, and judicial branches).
description_plain: Your child can describe how the U.S. government is organized into three branches: legislative, executive, and judicial.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of powers exercised by the federal government, tribal governments and state governments.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of what the federal, tribal, and state governments are allowed to do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of ways the Constitution limits the powers of the federal government.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how the Constitution limits what the federal government can do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the structure of government in the United States and how it functions.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the U.S. government is set up and how it works.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how rights guaranteed by the Constitution, including the Bill of Rights, and Democratic Values are involved in everyday situations.
description_plain: Your child can describe how rights in the Constitution and Bill of Rights affect everyday life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the principles of popular sovereignty, rule of law, checks and balances, separation of powers, and individual rights serve to limit the powers of the federal government as reflected in the Constitution and Bill of Rights.
description_plain: Your child can explain how ideas like voting, laws, and individual rights help limit the government’s power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the purposes of government as identified in the Preamble of the Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can describe why the government exists, as explained in the beginning of the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify questions political scientists ask in examining the United States.
description_plain: Your child can identify questions that political scientists ask about the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why people create governments.
description_plain: Your child can explain why people create governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess the positive and negative consequences of human activities on the physical environment of the United States and identify the causes of those activities.
description_plain: Your child can talk about how people’s actions help or harm the environment in the U.S. and explain why these actions happen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the effects of human-environment interactions.
description_plain: Your child understands how people’s actions affect the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe some of the movements of resources, goods, people, and information to, from, or within the United States, and explain the reasons for the movements.
description_plain: Your child can describe how and why resources, goods, people, and information move within, to, or from the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the impact of immigration to the United States on the cultural development of different places or regions of the United States.
description_plain: Your child can describe how immigration has changed the culture in different parts of the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a case study or story about migration within or to the United States to identify push and pull factors (why they left, why they came) that influenced the migration.
description_plain: Your child can use a story about people moving to or within the U.S. to explain why they left and why they came.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand how human activities help shape the Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child understands how what people do changes the land and places around them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate and describe human and physical characteristics of major U.S. regions and compare them to the Great Lakes region.
description_plain: Your child can find and describe important features of major U.S. regions and compare them to the Great Lakes area.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe ways in which the United States can be divided into different regions.
description_plain: Your child can describe different ways the United States can be divided into regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand how regions are created from common physical and human characteristics.
description_plain: Your child understands that regions are made based on things people and places have in common.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use hemispheres, continents, oceans, and major lines of latitude to describe the relative location of the United States on a world map.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and globes to show where the United States is in the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps to describe elevation, climate, and patterns of population density in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to describe the height of land, weather, and where people live in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the characteristics and purposes of a variety of technological geographic tools. answer geographic questions about the United States.
description_plain: Your child can use tools like maps and globes to answer questions about the geography of the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify questions geographers ask in examining the United States.
description_plain: Your child can identify questions that geographers ask about the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographic representations to acquire, process, and report information from a spatial perspective.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and other tools to find and share information about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe past and current threats to Michigan's natural resources and describe how state government, tribal and local governments, schools, organizations, and individuals worked in the past and continue to work today to protect its natural resources.
description_plain: Your child can describe problems Michigan’s natural resources have faced and how people and groups have worked to protect them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of primary and secondary sources to construct a historical narrative about the beginnings of the automobile industry and the labor movement in Michigan.
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to tell the story of how the car industry and labor movement started in Michigan.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use visual data and informational text or primary accounts to compare a major Michigan economic activity today with that same activity or a related activity in the past.
description_plain: Your child can compare a major Michigan business today with how it was in the past using pictures and information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the relationship between the location of natural resources and the location of industries (after 1837) affected and continue to affect the location and growth of Michigan cities.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the location of natural resources and industries has affected where Michigan cities are and how they have grown.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use case studies or stories to describe the ideas and actions of individuals involved in the Underground Railroad in Michigan and in the Great Lakes region.
description_plain: Your child can use stories to describe what people did in the Underground Railroad in Michigan and the Great Lakes region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use primary and secondary sources to explain how migration and immigration affected and continue to affect the growth of Michigan.
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to explain how people moving to Michigan have changed its growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use historical inquiry questions to investigate the development of Michigan's major economic activities from statehood to present.
description_plain: Your child can use questions about history to learn how Michigan’s main businesses have changed from statehood to now.

subject: Social Studies
description: The History of Michigan (Beyond Statehood)
description_plain: Your child understands the history of Michigan after it became a state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use democratic procedures to make decisions on civic issues in the school or classroom.
description_plain: Your child can use voting or group discussions to help make decisions about issues in their class or school.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different strategies students and others could take to address problems and predict possible results.
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways people can solve problems and can guess what might happen with each choice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess options for individuals and groups to plan and conduct activities intended to advance views on matters of public policy.
description_plain: Your child can look at different choices for taking action on public issues and decide which ones might help share their opinions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act out of the rule of law and hold others to the same standard.
description_plain: Your child understands the importance of following rules and expects others to do the same.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civic Participation
description_plain: Your child will learn how to take part in their community and make a difference.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the challenges people have faced and actions they have taken to address issues at different times and places.
description_plain: Your child can talk about problems people have faced in history and what they did to try to fix them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct an argument and justify a decision supported with evidence.
description_plain: Your child can make a case for a decision and back it up with facts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply Democratic Values or Constitutional Principles to support a position on an issue.
description_plain: Your child can use ideas about fairness and the Constitution to support their opinion on an issue.

subject: Social Studies
description: State an issue as a question of public policy and discuss possible solutions from different perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can ask a question about a public issue and talk about different ways to solve it from different points of view.

subject: Social Studies
description: Public Discourse And Decision Making
description_plain: Your child will learn how to talk about public issues and make group decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use data presented in social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts to answer compelling and supporting questions.
description_plain: Your child can use information from charts, graphs, maps, and texts to answer questions in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Know how to find relevant evidence from a variety of sources.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find useful information from different places to help answer questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use supporting questions to help answer compelling social studies questions.
description_plain: Your child can use smaller questions to help answer bigger social studies questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between compelling questions and supporting questions.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between main questions and supporting questions in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use compelling and supporting questions to investigate social studies problems.
description_plain: Your child can use main and supporting questions to explore social studies topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Inquiry, Research, And Analysis
description_plain: Your child will learn how to ask questions, do research, and analyze information in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify point of view and bias.
description_plain: Your child can spot when someone has a certain point of view or is being unfair in their information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express social science ideas or information in written, spoken, and graphic forms including tables, line graphs, bar graphs, and maps.
description_plain: Your child can share ideas and information by writing, speaking, or making charts, graphs, and maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between primary and secondary source documents.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between original documents and those that were created later.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use appropriate strategies to read and interpret basic social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts.
description_plain: Your child can use different strategies to read and understand charts, graphs, maps, and texts in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Reading And Communication – Read And Communicate Effectively
description_plain: Your child will learn how to read and communicate clearly about social studies topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the rights of individuals protected in the Bill of Rights (the first 10 amendments) to the U.S. Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can describe the rights that are protected for everyone in the first 10 amendments of the U.S. Constitution, called the Bill of Rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the concern that some people had about individual rights and why the inclusion of a Bill of Rights was needed for ratification.
description_plain: Your child can explain why some people wanted a Bill of Rights added to the Constitution to protect individual freedoms.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the principle of federalism and how it is expressed through the sharing and distribution of power as stated in the Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can describe how power is shared between the national and state governments in the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give reasons why the Framers wanted to limit the power of government.
description_plain: Your child can give reasons why the people who wrote the Constitution wanted to make sure the government didn't have too much power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the issues over representation and slavery the Framers faced at the Constitutional Convention and how they were addressed in the Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can explain the debates about representation and slavery at the Constitutional Convention and how they were solved in the Constitution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why the Constitutional Convention was convened and why the Constitution was written.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Constitutional Convention was held and why the Constitution was written.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of problems the country faced under the Articles of Confederation.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of problems the United States had under its first government, the Articles of Confederation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the powers of the national government and state governments under the Articles of Confederation.
description_plain: Your child can describe what powers the national and state governments had under the Articles of Confederation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain some of the challenges faced by the new nation under the Articles of Confederation, and analyze the development of the Constitution as a new plan for governing.
description_plain: Your child can explain the problems the new country faced under the Articles of Confederation and how the Constitution was created to solve them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the significance of the Treaty of Paris (establishment of the United States and its initial boundaries).
description_plain: Your child can describe why the Treaty of Paris was important and how it set the first borders of the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the role of women, enslaved and freed Africans, Indigenous Peoples, and France in helping shape the outcome of the war.
description_plain: Your child can find out how women, enslaved and free Africans, Indigenous Peoples, and France helped during the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the importance of Valley Forge, the Battle of Saratoga, and the Battle of Yorktown in the American Revolution.
description_plain: Your child can explain why Valley Forge, the Battle of Saratoga, and the Battle of Yorktown were important in the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the advantages and disadvantages each side had during the American Revolution with respect to military leadership, geography, types of resources, and motivations.
description_plain: Your child can describe the strengths and weaknesses each side had during the American Revolution, like leaders, land, resources, and reasons for fighting.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the multi-faceted nature of the American Revolution and its consequences.
description_plain: Your child can explain the different parts of the American Revolution and what happened because of it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify a problem that people in the colonies faced, identify alternative choices for addressing the problem with possible consequences, and describe the course of action taken.
description_plain: Your child can identify a problem colonists faced, suggest different ways they could have solved it, and explain what they actually did.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how colonial experiences with self-government and ideas about government influenced the decision to declare independence.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the colonists' experiences with self-government and their ideas about government led them to want independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the role that key individuals played in leading the colonists to revolution, including George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Benjamin Franklin, Patrick Henry, Samuel Adams, John Adams, and Thomas Paine.
description_plain: Your child can tell about important people who helped lead the colonists to the American Revolution, like George Washington and Thomas Jefferson.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use the Declaration of Independence to explain why many colonists wanted to separate from Great Britain and why they believed they had the right to do so.
description_plain: Your child can use the Declaration of Independence to explain why many colonists wanted to break away from Great Britain and felt they had the right to do so.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of the First and Second Continental Congresses in unifying the colonies.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the First and Second Continental Congresses helped bring the colonies together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using an event from the Revolutionary era, explain how British and colonial views on authority and the use of power without authority differed (views on representative government).
description_plain: Your child can explain how the British and colonists disagreed about who should have power and make decisions during the Revolutionary era.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the causes and effects of events such as the Stamp Act, the Boston Massacre, the Boston Tea Party, and the Intolerable Acts.
description_plain: Your child can describe what caused events like the Stamp Act, Boston Massacre, Boston Tea Party, and Intolerable Acts, and what happened because of them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the French and Indian War affected British policy toward the colonies and subsequent colonial dissatisfaction with the new policy.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the French and Indian War changed the way Britain treated the colonies and why this made colonists unhappy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the major political, economic, and ideological reasons for the American Revolution.
description_plain: Your child understands the main political, economic, and belief-based reasons why the American Revolution happened.

subject: Social Studies
description: Revolution And The New Nation (1754-1800)
description_plain: Your child will learn about the time of the American Revolution and the founding of the United States (1754-1800).

subject: Social Studies
description: Make generalizations about the reasons for regional differences in colonial America.
description_plain: Your child can explain why different regions in colonial America developed in different ways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the development of the emerging labor force in the colonies.
description_plain: Your child can describe how people started working and what kinds of jobs they did in the colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe colonial life in America from the perspectives of at least three different groups of people.
description_plain: Your child can describe what life was like in colonial America for at least three different groups of people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the daily lives of people living in the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies.
description_plain: Your child can describe what daily life was like for people living in the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies on a map.
description_plain: Your child can find the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish among and explain the reasons for regional differences in colonial America.
description_plain: Your child can explain how and why the different regions in colonial America were different from each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how enslaved and free Africans struggled to retain elements of their diverse African histories and cultures to develop distinct African-American identities.
description_plain: Your child can explain how enslaved and free Africans worked to keep their cultures and create a unique African-American identity.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the lives of enslaved Africans and free Africans, including fugitive and escaped slaves in the American colonies.
description_plain: Your child can describe what life was like for enslaved Africans and free Africans, including those who escaped slavery, in the colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe Triangular Trade, including:
  • the trade routes.
  • the people and goods that were traded.
  • the Middle Passage.
  • the impact on life in Africa.
description_plain: Your child can describe the Triangular Trade, including the trade routes, what and who was traded, the Middle Passage, and how it affected life in Africa.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the development of the slave system in the Americas and its impact.
description_plain: Your child understands how slavery developed in the Americas and what effects it had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the economic, political, cultural, and religious causes of migration to colonial North America.
description_plain: Your child can explain why people moved to colonial North America, including economic, political, cultural, and religious reasons.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the regional settlement patterns of the Southern colonies, New England, and the Middle colonies.
description_plain: Your child can compare how people settled in the Southern, New England, and Middle colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe significant developments in the Middle colonies, including:
  • patterns of settlement and control, including the impact of geography (landforms and climate) on settlement.
  • interactions with Indigenous Peoples, including the trading of goods, services, and ideas among Europeans and Indigenous Peoples.
  • the growth of economies in the Middle colonies, the Dutch settlement in New Netherlands, Quaker settlement in Pennsylvania, and subsequent English takeover of the Middle colonies.
  • immigration patterns leading to ethnic diversity in the Middle colonies.
description_plain: Your child can describe important events in the Middle colonies, such as how people settled, interacted with Indigenous Peoples, built their economies, and created diverse communities.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe significant developments in the New England colonies, including:
  • patterns of settlement and control including the impact of geography (landforms and climate) on settlement.
  • interactions with Indigenous Peoples, including the trading of goods, services, and ideas among Europeans and Indigenous Peoples, growth of agricultural (small farms) and non-agricultural (shipping, manufacturing) economies.
  • the development of government, including the establishment of town meetings, development of colonial legislatures, and growth of royal government.
  • religious tensions in Massachusetts that led to the establishment of other colonies in New England.
description_plain: Your child can describe important events in the New England colonies, including how people settled, worked with Indigenous Peoples, built their economies, formed governments, and dealt with religious disagreements.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe significant developments in the Southern colonies, including:
  • patterns of settlement and control, including the impact of geography (landforms and climate) on settlement.
  • the establishment of Jamestown.
  • the development of one-crop economies (plantation land use and growing season for rice in Carolinas and tobacco in Virginia).
  • interactions with Indigenous Peoples, including the trading of goods, services, and ideas among Europeans and Indigenous Peoples.
  • the development of colonial representative assemblies (House of Burgesses).
  • the development of slavery.
description_plain: Your child can describe important events in the Southern colonies, such as how people settled, started Jamestown, grew crops, interacted with Indigenous Peoples, formed governments, and began slavery.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the regional settlement patterns and describe significant developments in Southern, New England, and the Mid-Atlantic colonies.
description_plain: Your child can compare how people settled and what important things happened in the Southern, New England, and Mid-Atlantic colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: USHG Era 2 - Colonization And Settlement (1585-1763)
description_plain: Your child will learn about the time of colonization and settlement in America (1585-1763).

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the Columbian Exchange and its impact on Europeans, Indigenous Peoples, and Africans.
description_plain: Your child can describe the Columbian Exchange and how it changed the lives of Europeans, Indigenous Peoples, and Africans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the cultural impact that occurred between the British, French, and Spanish on the lives of Indigenous Peoples.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the British, French, and Spanish affected the cultures and lives of Indigenous Peoples.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use primary and secondary sources to compare Europeans, Africans, and Indigenous Peoples who converged in the Western Hemisphere after 1492 with respect to governmental structure, and views on property ownership and land use.
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to compare how Europeans, Africans, and Indigenous Peoples organized their governments and used land after 1492.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the convergence of Europeans, Indigenous Peoples, and Africans in the Americas after 1492 from the perspective of these three groups.
description_plain: Your child can describe what happened when Europeans, Indigenous Peoples, and Africans came together in the Americas after 1492, from each group's point of view.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the environmental, political, and cultural consequences of the interactions among European, African, and Indigenous Peoples in the late 15th century through the 17th century.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the environment, politics, and cultures changed when Europeans, Africans, and Indigenous Peoples interacted from the late 1400s to the 1600s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the life and cultural development of people living in West Africa before the 16th century with respect to economic (the ways people made a living) and family structures, and the growth of states, towns, and trade.
description_plain: Your child can describe what life was like in West Africa before the 1500s, including how people made a living, family life, and the growth of towns and trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps to locate the major regions of Africa (North Africa, West Africa, Central Africa, East Africa, Southern Africa).
description_plain: Your child can find the main regions of Africa (North, West, Central, East, and Southern Africa) on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the lives of peoples living in West Africa prior to the 16th century.
description_plain: Your child can describe what life was like for people living in West Africa before the 1500s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use case studies of individual explorers and stories of life in Europe to compare the goals, obstacles, motivations, and consequences for European exploration and colonization of the Americas.
description_plain: Your child can use stories about explorers and life in Europe to compare why Europeans explored and settled in the Americas, what challenges they faced, and what happened as a result.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the technological and political developments that made sea exploration possible.
description_plain: Your child can explain what new inventions and political changes made sea exploration possible.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the causes and consequences of European exploration and colonization.
description_plain: Your child can identify why Europeans explored and settled in the Americas and what happened because of it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe Eastern Woodland life with respect to governmental and family structures, trade, and their relationship to the land.
description_plain: Your child can describe how Eastern Woodland peoples organized their families and governments, traded, and related to the land.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare how Indigenous Peoples in the Eastern Woodland and another tribal region adapted to or modified the environment.
description_plain: Your child can compare how Indigenous Peoples in the Eastern Woodlands and another region changed or adapted to their environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps to locate peoples in the Eastern Woodland (the Woodland Peoples east of the Mississippi River), desert Southwest, the Pacific Northwest, and the nomadic nations of the Great Plains.
description_plain: Your child can find the Eastern Woodland, desert Southwest, Pacific Northwest, and Great Plains Indigenous Peoples on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the lives of the Indigenous Peoples living in North America prior to European contact.
description_plain: Your child can describe what life was like for Indigenous Peoples in North America before Europeans arrived.

subject: Social Studies
description: USHG Era 1 - Beginnings To 1620
description_plain: Your child will learn about the earliest times in American history, up to the year 1620.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in projects to help or inform others.
description_plain: Your child can take part in projects that help or teach others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop and implement an action plan and know how, when, and where to address or inform others about a public issue.
description_plain: Your child can make a plan to take action and knows how and when to talk to others about a public issue.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act constructively to further the public good.
description_plain: Your child can take positive actions to help their community or society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compose a short essay expressing a position on a contemporary public-policy issue related to the Constitution and justify the position with a reasoned argument.
description_plain: Your child can write a short essay sharing their opinion on a current issue related to the Constitution and explain their reasoning.

subject: Social Studies
description: Communicate a reasoned position on a public issue.
description_plain: Your child can clearly share their opinion on a public issue and explain why they think that way.

subject: Social Studies
description: Give examples of how conflicts over Democratic Values lead people to differ on contemporary Constitutional issues in the United States.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how disagreements about American values cause people to have different opinions on current Constitutional issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use graphic data and other sources to analyze information about a contemporary public issue related to the U.S. Constitution and evaluate alternative resolutions.
description_plain: Your child can use charts, graphs, and other information to study a current issue about the U.S. Constitution and consider different solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify contemporary public issues related to the U.S. Constitution and their related factual, definitional, and ethical questions.
description_plain: Your child can identify current issues related to the U.S. Constitution and ask questions about the facts, definitions, and ethics involved.

subject: Social Studies
description: Clearly state a problem as a public policy issue, analyze various perspectives, and generate and evaluate possible alternative resolutions.
description_plain: Your child can clearly describe a public policy problem, look at different viewpoints, and think about possible solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use democratic procedures to make decisions on civic issues in the school or classroom.
description_plain: Your child can use voting or other fair methods to help make decisions about issues in their class or school.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain different strategies students and others could take to address problems and predict possible results.
description_plain: Your child can explain different ways people can solve problems and guess what might happen as a result.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess options for individuals and groups to plan and conduct activities intended to advance views on matters of public policy.
description_plain: Your child can think about different ways people or groups can plan and do activities to support their opinions on public issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act out of the rule of law and hold others to the same standard.
description_plain: Your child acts by following the law and expects others to do the same.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civic Participation
description_plain: Your child participates in activities that help their community or school.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the challenges people have faced and actions they have taken to address issues at different times and places.
description_plain: Your child can explain problems people have faced in history and what they did to try to solve them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct an argument and justify a decision supported with evidence.
description_plain: Your child can make a decision, explain their choice, and support it with facts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply Democratic Values or Constitutional Principles to support a position on an issue.
description_plain: Your child can use American values or Constitutional ideas to support their opinion on an issue.

subject: Social Studies
description: State an issue as a question of public policy and discuss possible solutions from different perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can state a public policy issue as a question and discuss different solutions from various viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Public Discourse And Decision Making
description_plain: Your child learns how to talk about public issues and make decisions about them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use data presented in social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts to answer compelling and supporting questions.
description_plain: Your child can use information from tables, graphs, maps, and texts to answer important questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Know how to find relevant evidence from a variety of sources.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find useful information from different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use supporting questions to help answer compelling social studies questions.
description_plain: Your child can use smaller questions to help answer bigger social studies questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between compelling questions and supporting questions.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between main questions and supporting questions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use compelling and supporting questions to investigate social studies problems.
description_plain: Your child can use main and supporting questions to explore social studies topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Inquiry, Research, And Analysis
description_plain: Your child learns how to ask questions, do research, and analyze information in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify point of view and bias.
description_plain: Your child can recognize different points of view and spot bias in information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express social science ideas or information in written, spoken, and graphic forms including tables, line graphs, bar graphs, and maps.
description_plain: Your child can share social studies ideas or information by writing, speaking, or using charts, graphs, and maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Differentiate between primary and secondary source documents.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between original documents and those that describe or explain them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use appropriate strategies to read and interpret basic social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts.
description_plain: Your child can use different strategies to read and understand tables, graphs, maps, and texts in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Reading And Communication – Read And Communicate Effectively
description_plain: Your child learns how to read and communicate effectively about social studies topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in projects to help or inform others.
description_plain: Your child can take part in projects that help or teach others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Engage in activities intended to contribute to solving the local, national or global issues studied.
description_plain: Your child can join in activities that aim to solve problems in their community, country, or the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate knowledge of how, when, and where individuals would plan and conduct activities intended to advance views in matters of public policy, report the results, and evaluate effectiveness.
description_plain: Your child can explain how and when people plan and carry out activities to support their opinions on public issues, share what happened, and judge how well it worked.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act constructively to further the public good.
description_plain: Your child can take positive actions to help their community or society.

subject: Social Studies
description: develop an action plan to address or inform others about the issue at a local, national, or global scale.
description_plain: Your child can make a plan to take action or inform others about an issue at the local, national, or global level.

subject: Social Studies
description: compose a persuasive essay justifying a position with a reasoned argument.
description_plain: Your child can write a persuasive essay that explains and supports their opinion with good reasons.

subject: Social Studies
description: share and discuss findings of research and issue analysis in group discussions and debates.
description_plain: Your child can share and talk about their research and ideas in group discussions and debates.

subject: Social Studies
description: identify the causes and consequences and analyze the impact, both positive and negative.
description_plain: Your child can identify what causes an issue, what happens because of it, and look at both the good and bad effects.

subject: Social Studies
description: use Michigan social studies process and skills methods to acquire content knowledge and appropriate data about the issue.
description_plain: Your child can use social studies skills to gather information and facts about an issue.

subject: Social Studies
description: Integrate Michigan process and skills standards into a grade-appropriate project. Clearly state a global issue as a question of public policy, trace the origins of the issue, analyze various perspectives, and generate and evaluate alternative resolutions. Identify public policy issues related to global topics and issues studied. For example:
description_plain: Your child can do a project where they clearly state a global issue, find out where it started, look at different viewpoints, and think about possible solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identifying and analyzing issues, decision making, persuasive communication about a global issue, and civic participation
description_plain: Your child can identify and analyze global issues, make decisions, communicate their opinions, and participate as a citizen.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how communication innovations have affected economic interactions and where and how people work.
description_plain: Your child can explain how new ways of communicating have changed how people do business and where and how they work.

subject: Social Studies
description: Diagram or map the flow of materials, labor, and capital used to produce a consumer product.
description_plain: Your child can draw a diagram or map to show how materials, workers, and money are used to make a product people buy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use charts and graphs to compare imports and exports of different countries in the world and propose generalizations about patterns of economic interdependence.
description_plain: Your child can use charts and graphs to compare what different countries buy from and sell to each other, and notice patterns in how countries depend on one another.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe patterns and networks of economic interdependence, including trade.
description_plain: Your child understands how countries trade and rely on each other for goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the economic and ecological costs and benefits of different kinds of energy production.
description_plain: Your child can compare the good and bad effects on the economy and the environment of different ways to make energy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain and compare how economic systems (traditional, command, market) answer the three basic economic questions: What goods and services will be produced? How will they be produced? For whom will they be produced? Also, who will receive the benefits or bears the costs of production?
description_plain: Your child can explain and compare how different types of economies decide what to make, how to make it, who gets it, and who benefits or pays the costs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how societies organize to allocate resources to produce and distribute goods and services.
description_plain: Your child understands how societies decide how to use resources to make and share goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of sanctions, tariffs, treaties, quotas, and subsidies.
description_plain: Your child can explain how things like taxes on imports, trade limits, and agreements between countries affect economies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how national governments make decisions that affect the national economy.
description_plain: Your child understands how a country's government makes choices that impact its economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how incentives and disincentives in the market economy can change the decision-making process.
description_plain: Your child can explain how rewards or penalties in the economy can change the way people make decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how individuals, businesses, and government make economic decisions when confronting scarcity or surpluses in the market economy.
description_plain: Your child understands how people, businesses, and governments make choices when there is not enough or too much of something.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of treaties, agreements, and international organizations on global issues.
description_plain: Your child can explain how agreements and organizations between countries can affect global problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the challenges to governments to address global issues, and the international cooperation needed to do so.
description_plain: Your child can explain why it can be hard for governments to solve worldwide problems and why countries need to work together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how governments address national and international issues and form policies, and how the policies may not be consistent with those of other nation-states.
description_plain: Your child can explain how governments make rules to deal with problems in their own country and with other countries, and how these rules may be different from other countries’ rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the various ways that governments interact both positively and negatively.
description_plain: Your child can explain the different ways countries work together or disagree with each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast various forms of government around the world.
description_plain: Your child can compare and contrast different types of governments around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define the characteristics of modern nation-states.
description_plain: Your child can describe what makes a modern country, such as having borders, a government, and laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the characteristics of nation-states and how they may interact.
description_plain: Your child can describe what countries are like and how they interact with each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast different ideas about the purposes of government in different nations, nation-states or governments.
description_plain: Your child can compare different ideas about why governments exist in different countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how people identify, organize, and accomplish the purposes of government.
description_plain: Your child understands how people come together to create and run governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: develop an action plan to address or inform others about the issue, at local to global scales.
description_plain: Your child can make a plan to help solve or teach others about a problem, whether it’s local or global.

subject: Social Studies
description: compose a persuasive essay justifying a position with a reasoned argument.
description_plain: Your child can write a persuasive essay that explains and supports their opinion on an issue.

subject: Social Studies
description: share and discuss findings of research and issue analysis in group discussions and debates.
description_plain: Your child can share and talk about what they learned from research in group discussions or debates.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop a plan for action:
description_plain: Your child can come up with a plan to take action on an issue.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate a contemporary global issue by applying the skills of geographic inquiry.
description_plain: Your child can research a current world problem using geography skills.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify global issues.
description_plain: Your child can identify problems that affect people in many countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: A global issue is one that has an impact affecting many regions of the world (Global Topic Investigation and Issue Analysis)
description_plain: Your child understands that a global issue is a problem that affects many parts of the world.

subject: Social Studies
