description: Analyze how combinations of human decisions and natural forces can lead to (or help people avoid) a natural disaster.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's choices and natural events can cause or prevent natural disasters.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effects that a change in the physical environment could have on human activities and the actions people would be required to make (or would choose to make) in response to the change.
description_plain: Your child can explain how changes in the environment can affect what people do and how they respond.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how physical and human systems shape patterns on the Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child can describe how both nature and people shape the patterns we see on Earth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define natural resources and explain how people in different places use, define, and acquire resources in different ways.
description_plain: Your child can explain what natural resources are and how people in different places use and get them in different ways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways in which human-induced changes in the physical environment in one place can cause changes in other places.
description_plain: Your child can explain how changes people make to the environment in one place can affect other places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how different technologies can have positive and negative impacts on the environment.
description_plain: Your child can explain how technology can help or harm the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe examples of how humans have impacted and are continuing to impact the environment in different places as a consequence of population size, resource use, level of consumption, and technology.
description_plain: Your child can describe how people have changed the environment in different places because of population, resource use, and technology.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how humans use and modify the environment.
description_plain: Your child can describe how people use and change the environment to meet their needs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate examples of cooperation and conflict within the region under study from different perspectives.
description_plain: Your child can look at examples of people working together or having conflicts in a region and explain them from different points of view.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify factors that contribute to cooperation and conflict between and among cultural groups (control/use of natural resources, power, wealth, and cultural diversity).
description_plain: Your child can identify reasons why groups of people cooperate or have conflicts, such as over resources, power, wealth, or cultural differences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how forces of cooperation and conflict among people influence the division of the Earth's surface and its resources.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people working together or having conflicts affects how land and resources are divided.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the patterns, causes, and consequences of major human migrations.
description_plain: Your child can explain why large groups of people move from one place to another and what happens as a result.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe patterns of settlement and explain why people settle where they do and how people make their livings.
description_plain: Your child can describe where people live, why they choose those places, and how they make a living.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people have modified the environment and used technology to make places more suitable for humans, as well as how modifications sometimes have negative/unintended consequences.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people have changed the environment and used technology to make places better for living, and how these changes can sometimes cause problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe patterns, processes, and functions of human settlement.
description_plain: Your child can describe how and why people choose where to live and how communities grow and change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the advantages, disadvantages, and impacts of different technologies used to transport people and products, and spread ideas throughout the world.
description_plain: Your child can talk about the pros and cons of different ways people and goods move around the world, and how these methods help spread ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how technology creates patterns and networks that connect people, resources, products, and ideas.
description_plain: Your child understands how technology connects people, resources, products, and ideas across the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how culture influences the daily lives of people.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's culture affects what they do every day.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe cultures of the region being studied, including the major languages and religions.
description_plain: Your child can describe the main languages and religions of the region being studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the gender roles assigned to men and women in different societies.
description_plain: Your child can compare what is expected of men and women in different societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define culture and describe examples of cultural change through diffusion, including what has diffused, why and where it has spread, and positive and negative consequences of the change.
description_plain: Your child can explain what culture is and give examples of how parts of culture spread and change, including the good and bad effects.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the characteristics, distribution, and complexity of Earth's cultural mosaic.
description_plain: Your child can describe the different cultures around the world and how they are spread out and interact.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate major ecosystems and explain how and why they are similar or different as a consequence of latitude, elevation, land-forms, location, and human activity.
description_plain: Your child can find major ecosystems on a map and explain how things like location, height, land shapes, and people affect them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the characteristics and spatial distribution of ecosystems on Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child can describe what different ecosystems are like and where they are found on Earth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the factors that cause different climate types.
description_plain: Your child can explain what causes different types of climates around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret and compare climographs from different latitudes and locations.
description_plain: Your child can read and compare climate graphs from different places on Earth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the physical processes that shape the patterns of the Earth's surface.
description_plain: Your child can describe how natural forces like wind, water, and earthquakes shape the land.

subject: Social Studies
description: Generalize about how human and natural factors have influenced how people make a living and perform other activities in a place.
description_plain: Your child can explain how both nature and people affect the kinds of jobs and activities people do in a place.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret population pyramids from different countries including birth rates, death rates, male-female differences, and the causes and consequences of the age structure of the population.
description_plain: Your child can read population charts from different countries and explain what they show about age, gender, birth and death rates, and what these mean for the country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how culture and experience influence people's perceptions of places and regions.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people's culture and experiences shape how they see different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how communities are affected positively or negatively by changes in technology.
description_plain: Your child can explain how new technology can help or hurt communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the human characteristics of the region under study, including languages, religions, economic system, governmental system, cultural traditions.
description_plain: Your child can describe the people in a region, including their languages, religions, economy, government, and traditions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the human characteristics of places.
description_plain: Your child can describe what makes the people in a place unique, such as their language, traditions, and ways of life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate and describe the characteristics and patterns of major world climates and ecosystems.
description_plain: Your child can find and describe the main climates and ecosystems around the world and their patterns.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate and describe the basic patterns and processes of plate tectonics.
description_plain: Your child can find and describe where Earth's plates are and how they move to shape the land.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate and describe the basic patterns of landforms.
description_plain: Your child can find and describe the main types of landforms, like mountains and valleys, on a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the physical characteristics of places.
description_plain: Your child can describe what the land, water, and climate are like in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the different ways in which places are connected and how those connections demonstrate interdependence and accessibility.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different places are connected and depend on each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use the fundamental themes of geography (location, place, human-environment interaction, movement, region) to describe regions or places on earth.
description_plain: Your child can use basic ideas of geography, like location and movement, to describe places or regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create or interpret a map of the population distribution of a region and generalize about the factors influencing the distribution of the population.
description_plain: Your child can make or read a map showing where people live in a region and explain why people live where they do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate and use information from GIS and satellite remote sensing to answer geographic questions.
description_plain: Your child can use information from maps and satellites to answer questions about geography.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use images as the basis for answering geographic questions about the human and physical characteristics of places and major world regions.
description_plain: Your child can use pictures to answer questions about what places and regions are like.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use, interpret, and create maps and graphs representing population characteristics, natural features, and land use of the region under study.
description_plain: Your child can use, read, and make maps and graphs that show things like where people live, natural features, and how land is used.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why maps of the same place may vary, including the perspectives and purposes of the cartographers.
description_plain: Your child can explain why maps of the same place might look different depending on who made them and why.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply the skills of geographic inquiry (asking geographic questions, acquiring geographic information, organizing geographic information, analyzing geographic information, and answering geographic questions) to analyze a geographic problem or issue.
description_plain: Your child can ask and answer questions about geography by gathering and analyzing information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use skills of geographic inquiry and analysis to answer important questions about relationships between people, their cultures, and their environments, in their communities and within the larger world context. Students use information to make reasoned judgments based on the authenticity of the information, critically analyze the information, and present the results.
description_plain: Your child can use geography skills to answer questions about how people, cultures, and environments are connected, and can judge if information is trustworthy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Draw a sketch map, or add information to an outline map, of the world or a world region.
description_plain: Your child can draw or add details to a simple map of the world or a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of geographic tools (maps, globes, and web-based geography technology) to analyze the world at global, regional, and local scales.
description_plain: Your child can use maps, globes, and online tools to study the world at different levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use maps and other geographic tools to acquire and process information from a spatial perspective.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and other tools to find and understand information about places and spaces.

subject: Social Studies
description: Plan, conduct, and evaluate the effectiveness of activities intended to advance views on matters of public policy.
description_plain: Your child can plan and carry out activities to support their opinions about public issues, and see how well those activities work.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess options for individuals and groups to plan and conduct activities intended to advance views on matters of public policy.
description_plain: Your child can think about different ways people can work together to support their views on public issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act out of the rule of law and hold others to the same standard.
description_plain: Your child acts according to the law and expects others to do the same.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civic Participation
description_plain: Your child takes part in activities that help their community or country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the challenges people have faced and actions they have taken to address issues at different times and places.
description_plain: Your child can explain problems people have faced in history and what they did to try to solve them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct arguments expressing and justifying decisions on public policy issues supported with evidence.
description_plain: Your child can make and explain decisions about public issues, using facts to support their choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss public policy issues, clarifying position, considering opposing views, and applying Democratic Values or Constitutional Principles to develop and refine claims.
description_plain: Your child will talk about public issues, explain their opinion, listen to other viewpoints, and use American values or rules to improve their ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Clearly state an issue as a question of public policy, gather and interpret information about that issue, and generate and evaluate possible alternative resolutions.
description_plain: Your child can turn a public issue into a question, find and understand information about it, and come up with and judge different solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Public Discourse And Decision Making
description_plain: Your child understands how people talk about and make decisions on issues that affect the public.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use resources in multiple forms and from multiple perspectives to analyze issues.
description_plain: Your child can use different types of resources and viewpoints to better understand issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Know how to find, organize, and interpret information from a variety of sources.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find, organize, and make sense of information from many sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate data presented in social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts.
description_plain: Your child can understand and judge information shown in tables, graphs, maps, and texts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use compelling and supporting questions to investigate social scientific problems.
description_plain: Your child uses good questions to explore and learn about social studies problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Inquiry, Research, And Analysis
description_plain: Your child understands how to ask questions, do research, and analyze information in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Present an argument supported with evidence.
description_plain: Your child can make an argument and back it up with facts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express social science ideas clearly in written, spoken, and graphic forms, including tables, line graphs, bar graphs, pie charts, maps, and images.
description_plain: Your child can clearly share social studies ideas by writing, speaking, or using charts, graphs, maps, and pictures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret primary and secondary source documents for point of view, context, bias, and frame of reference or perspective.
description_plain: Your child can look at original and secondhand sources to understand their point of view, background, and possible bias.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use appropriate strategies to read and interpret basic social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts.
description_plain: Your child uses the right strategies to read and understand basic social studies charts, graphs, maps, and texts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Reading And Communication-Read And Communicate Effectively
description_plain: Your child can read and communicate social studies information clearly and effectively.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in projects to help or inform others.
description_plain: Your child will take part in projects that help or teach others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Engage in activities intended to contribute to solving a national or international problem studied.
description_plain: Your child will join activities that try to solve national or world problems they have studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate knowledge of how, when, and where individuals would plan and conduct activities intended to advance views in matters of public policy, report the results, and evaluate effectiveness.
description_plain: Your child can explain how and when people plan and carry out actions to support their views on public issues, share results, and judge how well it worked.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act constructively to further the public good.
description_plain: Your child will act in ways that help the community or society.

subject: Social Studies
description: develop an action plan to address or inform others about the issue at the different scales.
description_plain: Your child can make a plan to address or inform others about an issue at different levels (local, national, or global).

subject: Social Studies
description: compose a persuasive essay justifying the position with a reasoned argument.
description_plain: Your child can write a convincing essay that explains and supports their opinion.

subject: Social Studies
description: share and discuss findings of research and issue analysis in group discussions and debates.
description_plain: Your child can share and talk about their research and analysis in group discussions and debates.

subject: Social Studies
description: identify the causes and consequences and analyze the impact, both positive and negative.
description_plain: Your child can find out what caused an issue, what happened because of it, and analyze both the good and bad effects.

subject: Social Studies
description: use inquiry methods to acquire content knowledge and appropriate data about the issue.
description_plain: Your child uses research methods to learn facts and gather information about an issue.

subject: Social Studies
description: clearly state the issue as a question of public policy orally or in written form.
description_plain: Your child can clearly state a public policy issue as a question, either by speaking or writing.

subject: Social Studies
description: identify public policy issues related to global topics and issues studied.
description_plain: Your child can identify public policy issues related to global topics they have studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Clearly state an issue as a question of public policy in contemporary or historical context, or as a contemporary/historical comparison. Trace the origins of an issue, analyze and synthesize various perspectives, and generate and evaluate alternative resolutions. Deeply examine policy issues in group discussions and debates to make reasoned and informed decisions. Write persuasive/argumentative essays expressing and justifying decisions on public policy issues. Plan and conduct activities intended to advance views on matters of public policy, report the results, and evaluate effectiveness:
description_plain: Your child can clearly state a public policy issue, explore its background, consider different viewpoints, come up with and judge solutions, discuss it in groups, write a persuasive essay about it, and plan and carry out actions to address it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and analyse issues, decision making, persuasive communication about a public issue, and citizen involvement
description_plain: Your child can find and analyze issues, make decisions, communicate persuasively about public issues, and understand how citizens can get involved.

subject: Social Studies
description: use a case study to describe the culture and economy of Indigenous Peoples in North America prior to 1500.
description_plain: Your child can use a real-life example to describe the culture and economy of Indigenous Peoples in North America before 1500.

subject: Social Studies
description: use a case study to describe how trade integrated cultures and influenced the economy within early African empires.
description_plain: Your child can use a real-life example to explain how trade connected cultures and affected the economy in early African empires.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyze the environmental, economic, and political crises in the classical world that led to the collapse of classical empires and the consolidation of Byzantium.
description_plain: Your child can explain how environmental, economic, and political problems led to the fall of classical empires and the rise of Byzantium.

subject: Social Studies
description: WHG Era 4: Bridge To Era 4-Case Studies From Three Continents
description_plain: Your child will study important examples from three continents to understand changes in world history during this era.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate the geographical center of major religions and map the spread through 1500 CE.
description_plain: Your child can find where major religions started and show how they spread up to the year 1500.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the core beliefs of major world religions and belief systems, including Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, Confucianism, Sikhism and Islam.
description_plain: Your child can name and describe the main beliefs of major world religions like Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, Confucianism, Sikhism, and Islam.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how world religions or belief systems of Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Christianity, Confucianism, Sikhism, and Islam grew and their significance (Sikhism and Islam are included here even though they came after 300 CE). The world's major faiths and ethical systems emerged, establishing institutions, systems of thought, and cultural styles that would influence neighboring peoples and endure for centuries.
description_plain: Your child can explain how major world religions grew, why they are important, and how they shaped cultures and ideas for many years.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of economics in shaping the development of classical civilizations and empires.
description_plain: Your child can explain how money and trade affected the growth of classical civilizations and empires.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create a timeline that illustrates the rise and fall of classical empires during the classical period.
description_plain: Your child can make a timeline showing when classical empires rose and fell.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the significance of legal codes, belief systems, written languages, and communications in the development of large regional empires.
description_plain: Your child can describe why laws, beliefs, writing, and communication were important for building large empires.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of state authority, military power, taxation systems, 71 and institutions of coerced labor, including slavery, in building and maintaining empires.
description_plain: Your child can explain how government power, armies, taxes, and forced labor (like slavery) helped build and keep empires.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a case study to describe how trade integrated cultures and influenced the economy within empires.
description_plain: Your child can use a real-life example to show how trade brought cultures together and affected the economy in empires.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use historic and modern maps to locate and describe trade networks linking empires in the classical era.
description_plain: Your child can use old and new maps to find and describe trade routes that connected empires in the classical era.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe major achievements from Indian, Chinese, Mediterranean, African, Southwest and Central Asian, Mesoamerican, and Andean civilizations.
description_plain: Your child can describe important accomplishments from ancient Indian, Chinese, Mediterranean, African, Southwest and Central Asian, Mesoamerican, and Andean civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess the importance of Greek ideas about democracy and citizenship in the development of Western political thought and institutions.
description_plain: Your child understands why Greek ideas about democracy and citizenship are important for how governments work today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the defining characteristics of a city-state, civilization, and empire.
description_plain: Your child can explain the differences and similarities between a city-state, a civilization, and an empire.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using historic and modern maps, locate three major empires of this era, describe their geographic characteristics including physical features and climates, and propose a generalization about the relationship between geographic characteristics and the development of early empires.
description_plain: Your child can find three major ancient empires on maps, describe their land and climate, and explain how geography affected their growth.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the characteristics that classical civilizations share.
description_plain: Your child can describe what classical civilizations have in common.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze classical civilizations and empires and their lasting impact.
description_plain: Your child understands how ancient civilizations and empires shaped the world and still influence us today.

subject: Social Studies
description: WHG Era 3: Classical Traditions, World Religions, And Major Empires, 1000 BCE To 300 CE
description_plain: Your child will learn about ancient traditions, world religions, and major empires from 1000 BCE to 300 CE.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe pastoralism and explain how the climate and geography of Central Asia were linked to the rise of pastoral societies on the steppes.
description_plain: Your child can explain what pastoralism is and how the land and weather in Central Asia helped create societies that raised animals on the grasslands.

subject: Social Studies
description: Define the concept of cultural diffusion and explain how ideas and technology spread from one region to another.
description_plain: Your child understands what cultural diffusion is and can explain how ideas and technology spread from one place to another.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine early civilizations to describe their common features, including environment, economies, and social institutions.
description_plain: Your child can describe what early civilizations had in common, like their environment, economies, and social structures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use historical and modern maps and other sources to locate, describe, and analyze major river systems and discuss the ways these physical settings supported permanent settlements and development of early civilizations.
description_plain: Your child can use maps to find and describe major river systems and explain how these rivers helped early people settle and build civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the invention of agriculture led to the emergence of agrarian civilizations (seasonal harvests, specialized crops, cultivation, and development of villages and towns).
description_plain: Your child can explain how farming led to the growth of villages and towns, with people growing crops and harvesting at certain times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the importance of the development of human communication (oral, visual, and written) and its relationship to the development of culture.
description_plain: Your child can describe why the development of speaking, writing, and other forms of communication was important for building cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze early civilizations and pastoral societies.
description_plain: Your child understands how early civilizations and groups who raised animals shaped history.

subject: Social Studies
description: WHG Era 2: Early Civilizations And Cultures And The Emergence Of Pastoral Peoples, 4000 To 1000 BCE And Western Hemisphere 4000 BCE to 1500 CE
description_plain: Your child will learn about early civilizations, cultures, and the rise of people who lived by herding animals from 4000 to 1000 BCE and in the Americas up to 1500 CE.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the impact of the first Agricultural Revolution (stable food supply, surplus, population growth, trade, division of labor, development of settlements, changes to the environment, and changes to hunter-gatherer societies).
description_plain: Your child can explain how the first Agricultural Revolution changed life by providing more food, helping populations grow, starting trade, and leading to new jobs and settlements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the importance of the natural environment in the development of agricultural settlements in different locations.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the natural environment was important for starting farming communities in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the transition of many cultures from hunter-gatherers to sedentary agriculture (domestication of plants and animals).
description_plain: Your child can describe how many groups changed from hunting and gathering to farming and raising animals.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the Agricultural Revolution and explain why it was a turning point in history.
description_plain: Your child can describe the Agricultural Revolution and explain why it was a major turning point in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain what archaeologists have learned about Paleolithic and Neolithic societies.
description_plain: Your child can explain what archaeologists have learned about people who lived during the Old Stone Age and New Stone Age.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how and when human communities populated major regions of the world and adapted to a variety of environments.
description_plain: Your child can explain how and when people moved to different parts of the world and adapted to new environments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the spread of people during Era 1.
description_plain: Your child can describe how people spread out across the world in early times.

subject: Social Studies
description: WHG Era 1: The Beginnings Of Human Society: Beginnings To 4000 BCE
description_plain: Your child will learn about the beginnings of human society up to 4000 BCE.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use historical perspectives to analyze global issues faced by humans long ago and today.
description_plain: Your child can use what they've learned about history to think about problems people faced long ago and today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and use themes of history to study patterns of change and continuity.
description_plain: Your child can use big ideas from history to study how things have changed or stayed the same over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and use cultural institutions to study an era and a region.
description_plain: Your child can use information about cultures, like schools or religions, to study a certain time and place.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use historical concepts, patterns, and themes to study the past.
description_plain: Your child can use important ideas and patterns from history to help understand the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the role of the individual in history and the significance of one person's ideas.
description_plain: Your child can explain how one person's ideas or actions made a difference in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how historians use methods of inquiry to identify cause/effect relationships in history, noting that many have multiple causes.
description_plain: Your child can describe how historians figure out what caused events in history, knowing that there can be more than one cause.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and evaluate differing historical perspectives based on evidence.
description_plain: Your child can compare different points of view about history using evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the point of view (perspective of the author) and context when reading and discussing primary and secondary sources.
description_plain: Your child can identify who wrote a historical source and the situation in which it was created.

subject: Social Studies
description: Read and comprehend a historical passage to identify basic factual knowledge and the literal meaning by indicating who was involved, what happened, where it happened, what events led to the development, and what consequences or outcomes followed.
description_plain: Your child can read a historical passage and find out who was involved, what happened, where and why it happened, and what happened next.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how historians use a variety of sources to explore the past.
description_plain: Your child can explain how historians use different sources to learn about the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use historical inquiry and analysis to study the past.
description_plain: Your child can use questions and research to study and understand history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast several different calendar systems used in the past and present and their cultural significance.
description_plain: Your child can compare different types of calendars used in history and explain why they mattered to different cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use historical conceptual devices to organize and study the past.
description_plain: Your child can use tools like timelines and calendars to help organize and study history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people defined and used natural resources in the era being studied.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people used and valued natural resources in the time period being studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how different technologies were used in the era being studied.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people used different technologies in the time period being studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe examples of how humans modified the environment in the era being studied.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of how people changed the environment in the time period being studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how humans used, adapted to, and modified the environment in the era studied.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people used, adapted to, and changed their environment during the time period studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe examples of cooperation and conflict in the era being studied.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of times when groups worked together or disagreed during the time period being studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify factors that contribute to conflict and cooperation between and among cultural groups.
description_plain: Your child can point out reasons why groups might get along or have conflicts with each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how forces of conflict and cooperation among people influence the division of the Earth's surface and its resources.
description_plain: Your child understands how people working together or fighting can affect how land and resources are divided.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the patterns, causes, and consequences of major human migrations.
description_plain: Your child can explain why large groups of people moved from one place to another, and what happened because of those moves.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe patterns of settlement and explain why people settled where they did.
description_plain: Your child can describe where people chose to live and explain why they picked those places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people in the past have modified the environment and used technology to make places more suitable for humans.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people in the past changed their surroundings and used inventions to make life easier.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe patterns, processes, and functions of human settlement.
description_plain: Your child can describe how and why people form communities and live in certain areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the advantages, disadvantages, and impacts of different technologies used to transport products and ideas in the era being studied.
description_plain: Your child can talk about different ways people moved goods and ideas in the past, and the good and bad effects of those methods.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how technology creates patterns and networks that connect people, products, and ideas.
description_plain: Your child understands how technology helps connect people, products, and ideas across different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: conduct research on topics and issues, compose persuasive essays, and develop a plan for action.
description_plain: Your child can research a topic, write a persuasive essay, and make a plan to take action on an issue.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigation And Analysis (P1, P2)
description_plain: Your child can investigate and analyze topics or problems using research skills.

subject: Social Studies
description: Locate and use information from maps and GIS to answer geographic questions on the era and region being studied.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and digital mapping tools to find information and answer questions about the time and place being studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use, interpret, and create maps and graphs representing places and regions in the era being studied.
description_plain: Your child can use, understand, and make maps and graphs to show information about places from the time period being studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply the skills of geographic inquiry (asking geographic questions, acquiring geographic information, organizing geographic information, analyzing geographic information, and answering geographic questions) to analyze a geographic problem or issue.
description_plain: Your child can ask questions about geography, gather information, organize it, and use it to solve a problem.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use a variety of geographical tools (maps, globes, geographic information systems [GIS], and web-based geography technology) to analyze what is happening at different times in different locations.
description_plain: Your child can use maps, globes, and online geography tools to understand what is happening in different places and times.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use geographical inquiry and analysis to answer questions about relationships between peoples, cultures, and their environments, and interaction among places and cultures within the era under study.
description_plain: Your child can use geography skills to answer questions about how people, cultures, and environments interact in the time period being studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Plan, conduct, and evaluate the effectiveness of activities intended to advance views on matters of public policy.
description_plain: Your child can plan, carry out, and judge how well activities work to support their opinions on public issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess options for individuals and groups to plan and conduct activities intended to advance views on matters of public policy.
description_plain: Your child can look at different ways people can work together to support their views on public issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act out of the rule of law and hold others to the same standard.
description_plain: Your child understands the importance of following the law and expects others to do the same.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civic Participation
description_plain: Your child can take part in activities that help their community or government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the challenges people have faced and actions they have taken to address issues at different times and places.
description_plain: Your child can explain problems people have faced and what they did to try to solve them in different times and places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct arguments expressing and justifying decisions on public policy issues supported with evidence.
description_plain: Your child can make and support arguments about public issues using facts and evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss public policy issues, clarifying position, considering opposing views, and applying Democratic Values or Constitutional Principles to develop and refine claims.
description_plain: Your child can talk about public issues, explain their opinion, listen to other views, and use American values to shape their ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Clearly state an issue as a question of public policy, gather and interpret information about that issue, and generate and evaluate possible alternative resolutions.
description_plain: Your child can turn a public issue into a question, gather and understand information about it, and think about different solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Public Discourse And Decision Making
description_plain: Your child can discuss and make decisions about public issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use resources in multiple forms and from multiple perspectives to analyze issues.
description_plain: Your child can use different types of resources and viewpoints to study an issue.

subject: Social Studies
description: Know how to find, organize, and interpret information from a variety of sources.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find, organize, and understand information from many sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate data presented in social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts.
description_plain: Your child can understand and judge information shown in tables, graphs, maps, and texts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use compelling and supporting questions to investigate social scientific problems.
description_plain: Your child can ask good questions to help investigate social studies problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Inquiry, Research, And Analysis
description_plain: Your child can research and analyze information to answer questions about social studies topics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Present an argument supported with evidence.
description_plain: Your child can make an argument and back it up with evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express social science ideas clearly in written, spoken, and graphic forms, including tables, line graphs, bar graphs, pie charts, maps, and images.
description_plain: Your child can clearly share ideas in writing, speaking, and by using charts, graphs, maps, and pictures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret primary and secondary source documents for point of view, context, bias, and frame of reference or perspective.
description_plain: Your child can understand and explain the point of view and background of different documents from history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use appropriate strategies to read and interpret basic social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts.
description_plain: Your child can read and understand basic tables, graphs, maps, and texts in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Reading And Communication-Read And Communicate Effectively
description_plain: Your child can read and communicate ideas about social studies clearly.

subject: Social Studies
description: use historical perspectives to analyze issues in the United States from the past and the present; conduct research on a historical issue or topic, identify a connection to a contemporary issue, and present findings (e.g., oral, visual, video, or electronic presentation, persuasive essay, or research paper); include causes and consequences of the historical action and predict possible consequences of the contemporary action.
description_plain: Your child can study a historical issue in the U.S., connect it to a current issue, and share what they learned, including causes, effects, and possible future outcomes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use the historical perspective to investigate a significant historical topic from U.S. History Eras 3-6 that also has significance as an issue or topic in the United States today.
description_plain: Your child can use history to study an important topic from the past that still matters today.

subject: Social Studies
description:
America at Century's End-compare and contrast the United States in 1800 with the United States in 1898, focusing on similarities and differences in:
  • territory.
  • population.
  • systems of transportation.
  • governmental policies promoting economic development.
  • economic change.
  • the treatment of African-Americans.
  • the policies toward Indigenous Peoples.
description_plain: Your child can compare what the United States was like in 1800 and 1898, looking at land, people, transportation, economic growth, and how different groups were treated.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the major changes in communication, transportation, demography, and urban centers, including the location and growth of cities linked by industry and trade, in the last half of the 19th century.
description_plain: Your child can explain big changes in communication, transportation, population, and city growth in the late 1800s.

subject: Social Studies
description: USHG Era 6: The Development Of An Industrial, Urban, And Global United States (1870-1930)
description_plain: Your child understands how the United States became more industrial, urban, and connected to the world between 1870 and 1930.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the decision to remove Union troops from the South in 1877 and investigate its impact on Americans.
description_plain: Your child can explain why Union soldiers left the South in 1877 and how this affected people living in America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the intent and the effect of the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Amendments to the Constitution.
description_plain: Your child understands what the 13th, 14th, and 15th Amendments were meant to do and how they changed life in America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the new role of African-Americans in local, state, and federal government in the years after the Civil War and the national and regional resistance to this change, including the Ku Klux Klan.
description_plain: Your child can describe how African-Americans began to take part in government after the Civil War and how some people and groups, like the Ku Klux Klan, tried to stop this.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe the early responses to the end of the Civil War by describing:
  • the policies of the Freedmen's Bureau.
  • the restrictions placed on the rights and opportunities of freedmen, including racial segregation and Black Codes.
description_plain: Your child can describe how people responded after the Civil War, including what the Freedmen's Bureau did and how freed Black people still faced unfair rules and segregation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the different positions concerning the reconstruction of Southern society and the nation, including the positions of President Abraham Lincoln, President Andrew Johnson, Republicans, Democrats, and African-Americans.
description_plain: Your child can compare what different groups and leaders, like Lincoln, Johnson, Republicans, Democrats, and African-Americans, wanted for rebuilding the South and the country after the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Using evidence, develop an argument regarding the character and consequences of Reconstruction.
description_plain: Your child can use facts to make an argument about what Reconstruction was like and what happened because of it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct generalizations about how the war affected combatants, civilians (including the role of women and Indigenous Peoples), the physical environment, and the future of warfare, including technological developments.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Civil War affected soldiers, regular people (including women and Indigenous Peoples), the land, and how wars were fought in the future.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the role of African-Americans in the war, including black soldiers and regiments, and the increased resistance of enslaved people.
description_plain: Your child can describe the important roles African-Americans played in the Civil War, including as soldiers and in resisting slavery.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine Abraham Lincoln's presidency with respect to:
  • his military and political leadership.
  • the evolution of his emancipation policy (including the Emancipation Proclamation).
  • The role of his significant writings and speeches, including the Gettysburg Address and its relationship to the Declaration of Independence.
description_plain: Your child can explain what Abraham Lincoln did as a leader during the Civil War, how his views on freeing enslaved people changed, and why his speeches and writings were important.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Make an argument to explain the reasons why the North won the Civil War by considering the following:
  • critical events and battles in the war.
  • the political and military leadership of the North and South.
  • respective advantages and disadvantages of each side, including geographic, demographic, economic, and technological.
description_plain: Your child can make a case for why the North won the Civil War by looking at important battles, leaders, and the strengths and weaknesses of both sides.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the social, political, economic, and cultural reasons for secession.
description_plain: Your child can talk about the different reasons—social, political, economic, and cultural—why Southern states decided to leave the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the multiple causes, key events, and complex consequences of the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child can look at the many causes, big events, and results of the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how major issues debated at the Constitutional Convention, such as disagreements over the distribution of political power, rights of individuals (liberty and property), rights of states, the election of the executive, and slavery, help explain the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child can explain how disagreements at the Constitutional Convention, like those about power, rights, and slavery, helped lead to the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the resistance of enslaved persons and effects of their actions before and during the Civil War.
description_plain: Your child can describe how enslaved people resisted slavery and what happened because of their actions before and during the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Draw conclusions about why the following increased sectional tensions:
  • the Missouri Compromise (1820).
  • the Wilmot Proviso (1846).
  • the Compromise of 1850, including the Fugitive Slave Act.
  • the Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854) and subsequent conflict in Kansas.
  • the Dred Scott v. Sandford decision (1857).
  • changes in the party system.
description_plain: Your child can explain why certain laws and events, like the Missouri Compromise and the Dred Scott decision, made tensions between the North and South worse.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the competing views of John C. Calhoun, Daniel Webster, and Henry Clay on the nature of the union among the states.
description_plain: Your child can describe the different opinions of John C. Calhoun, Daniel Webster, and Henry Clay about how the states should work together as a country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the impact of the Northwest Ordinance on the expansion of slavery.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Northwest Ordinance affected the spread of slavery in the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the differences in the lives of free black people (including those who escaped from slavery) with the lives of free white people and enslaved people.
description_plain: Your child can compare what life was like for free Black people, free white people, and enslaved people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze and evaluate the early attempts to abolish or contain slavery and to realize the ideals of the Declaration of Independence.
description_plain: Your child can look at early efforts to end or limit slavery and how people tried to live up to the ideas in the Declaration of Independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: USHG Era 5: Civil War And Reconstruction (1850-1877)
description_plain: Your child understands the main events and changes in America during the Civil War and Reconstruction (1850-1877).

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate the role of religion in shaping antebellum reform movements.
description_plain: Your child can find out how religion influenced people who wanted to change society before the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the goals and effects of the antebellum temperance movement.
description_plain: Your child can explain what the temperance movement was trying to do and what effects it had.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the antebellum women's rights (and suffrage) movement by discussing the goals of its leaders and comparing primary source documents from this era to the Declaration of Independence.
description_plain: Your child can talk about what leaders of the women's rights movement wanted before the Civil War and compare their ideas to the Declaration of Independence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the formation and development of the abolitionist movement by considering the roles of key abolitionist leaders and the response of southerners and northerners to the abolitionist movement.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the movement to end slavery started and grew, who the key leaders were, and how people in the North and South reacted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the origins of the American education system.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the American school system began.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the growth of antebellum American reform movements.
description_plain: Your child can look at how different reform movements grew in America before the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: develop an argument based on evidence about the positive and negative consequences of territorial and economic expansion on Indigenous Peoples, efforts to maintain and sustain the institution of slavery, and the relations between free and slave-holding states.
description_plain: Your child can use facts to explain both the good and bad effects of expanding land and the economy on Indigenous Peoples, slavery, and the relationship between free and slave states.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyze the annexation of the west through the Louisiana Purchase, the removal of Indigenous Peoples from their ancestral homelands, the Mexican-American War, the growth of a system of commercial agriculture, and the idea of Manifest Destiny.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the United States grew westward, including buying land, moving Indigenous Peoples, fighting the Mexican-American War, growing new farms, and the idea of Manifest Destiny.

subject: Social Studies
description: explain the ideology of the institution of slavery, its policies, and consequences.
description_plain: Your child can explain the beliefs, rules, and effects of slavery in America.

subject: Social Studies
description:
compare and contrast the social and economic systems of the Northeast, the South, and the Western Frontier (Kentucky, Ohio Valley, etc.) with respect to geography, climate, and the development of:
  • agriculture, including changes in productivity, technology, supply and demand, and price.
  • industry, including the entrepreneurial development of new industries, such as textiles.
  • the labor force, including labor incentives and changes in labor forces.
  • transportation, including changes in transportation (steamboats and canal barges) and the impact on economic markets and prices.
  • immigration and the growth of nativism.
  • race relations.
  • class relations.
description_plain: Your child can compare how the Northeast, South, and Western Frontier were different in farming, industry, jobs, transportation, immigration, and relationships between races and social classes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and analyze the nature and impact of territorial, demographic, and economic growth in the first three decades of the new nation, using maps, charts, and other evidence.
description_plain: Your child can describe and analyze how America grew in land, population, and economy in its early years, using maps and charts.

subject: Social Studies
description: use Marbury v. Madison to explain the development of the power of the Supreme Court through the doctrine of judicial review.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Supreme Court gained more power through the Marbury v. Madison case.

subject: Social Studies
description: examine the origins and intentions of early American political parties, including how they emerged, who participated, and what influenced their ideologies.
description_plain: Your child can describe how the first American political parties started, who joined them, and what they believed.

subject: Social Studies
description: assess the changes in America's relationships with other nations by analyzing the origins, intents, and purposes of treaties.
description_plain: Your child can explain how America's relationships with other countries changed by looking at the reasons for and goals of different treaties.

subject: Social Studies
description: use President George Washington's farewell address to analyze Washington's perspective on the most significant challenges the new nation faced.
description_plain: Your child can use George Washington's farewell address to understand what he thought were the biggest problems facing the new country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the challenges the new federal government faced and the roles of political and social leaders in meeting those challenges.
description_plain: Your child can analyze the problems the new U.S. government faced and how leaders tried to solve them.

subject: Social Studies
description: USHG Era 4: Expansion And Reform (1792-1861)
description_plain: Your child understands the main events and changes in America during the time of expansion and reform (1792-1861).

subject: Social Studies
description: Use important ideas and documents to describe the philosophical origins of constitutional government in the United States with an emphasis on the following ideals: social contract, limited government, natural rights, right of revolution, separation of powers, bicameralism, republicanism, and popular participation in government.
description_plain: Your child can use important ideas and documents to explain where the U.S. government got its main principles, like limited power, rights, and participation by the people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the Bill of Rights reflected the concept of limited government, protection of basic freedoms, and the fear among many Americans of a strong central government.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Bill of Rights was meant to protect people's freedoms and limit the government's power.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the debates over the ratification of the Constitution from the perspectives of Federalists and Anti-Federalists and describe how the states ratified the Constitution.
description_plain: Your child can describe the arguments for and against the Constitution from both Federalists and Anti-Federalists, and explain how the states agreed to it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the new Constitution resolved (or compromised) the major issues, including sharing and separation of power and checking of power among federal government institutions; dual sovereignty (state-federal power); rights of individuals; the Electoral College; the Three-Fifths Compromise; the Great Compromise; and relationships and affairs with tribal nations.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Constitution solved or compromised on big issues like sharing power between states and the federal government, protecting individual rights, the Electoral College, and agreements like the Three-Fifths and Great Compromises, as well as how it dealt with Native American nations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the major issues debated at the Constitutional Convention, including the distribution of political power among the states and within the federal government, the conduct of foreign affairs, commerce with tribes, rights of individuals, the election of the executive, and the enslavement of Africans as a regional and federal issue.
description_plain: Your child can describe the main arguments at the Constitutional Convention, such as how power should be divided, how to handle foreign affairs and trade with tribes, protecting people's rights, choosing the president, and the issue of slavery.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify economic, political, and cultural issues facing the nation during the period of the Articles of Confederation and the opening of the Constitutional Convention.
description_plain: Your child can identify the economic, political, and cultural problems the country faced during the Articles of Confederation and at the start of the Constitutional Convention.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the reasons for the adoption and subsequent failure of the Articles of Confederation.
description_plain: Your child can explain why the Articles of Confederation were created and why they eventually failed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the challenges faced by the new nation and analyze the development of the Constitution as a new plan for governing
description_plain: Your child can explain the problems the new nation faced and how the Constitution was developed as a new way to govern the country.

subject: Social Studies
description: USHG Era 3: Revolution And The New Nation
description_plain: Your child understands the key events and changes during the American Revolution and the founding of the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe the consequences of the American Revolution by analyzing and evaluating the relative influences of:
  • establishment of an independent republican government.
  • creation of the Articles of Confederation.
  • changing views on freedom and equality.
  • concerns over the distribution of power within government, between government and the governed, and among people.
description_plain: Your child can describe what happened after the American Revolution, including starting a new government, creating the Articles of Confederation, changing ideas about freedom and equality, and concerns about how power was shared.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Using the Declaration of Independence, including the grievances at the end of the document, describe the role this document played in expressing:
  • colonists' views of government.
  • their reasons for separating from Great Britain.
description_plain: Your child can use the Declaration of Independence to explain what the colonists thought about government and why they wanted to break away from Great Britain.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe the ideas, experiences, and interactions that influenced the colonists' decisions to declare independence by analyzing:
  • colonial ideas about government.
  • experiences with self-government.
description_plain: Your child can describe the ideas and experiences that led colonists to want independence, including their beliefs about government and their practice of self-government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Plan, conduct, and evaluate the effectiveness of activities intended to advance views on matters of public policy.
description_plain: Your child can plan, carry out, and judge how effective activities are for supporting their opinions on public issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Assess options for individuals and groups to plan and conduct activities intended to advance views on matters of public policy.
description_plain: Your child can look at different ways people can organize and do activities to support their views on public issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Act out of the rule of law and hold others to the same standard.
description_plain: Your child will follow the law and expect others to do the same.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civic Participation
description_plain: Your child understands how to take part in civic activities and community life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the challenges people have faced and actions they have taken to address issues at different times and places.
description_plain: Your child can explain problems people have faced in different times and places and what they did to solve them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct arguments expressing and justifying decisions on public policy issues supported with evidence.
description_plain: Your child can make and support arguments about public issues using evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss public policy issues, clarifying position, considering opposing views, and applying Democratic Values or Constitutional Principles to develop and refine claims.
description_plain: Your child can talk about public issues, explain their own view, consider other opinions, and use American values or principles to improve their ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Clearly state an issue as a question of public policy, gather and interpret information about that issue, and generate and evaluate possible alternative resolutions.
description_plain: Your child can clearly state a public issue as a question, gather and understand information about it, and think about different possible solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Public Discourse And Decision Making
description_plain: Your child understands how people discuss and make decisions about public issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use resources in multiple forms and from multiple perspectives to analyze issues.
description_plain: Your child can use different types of resources and viewpoints to study issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Know how to find, organize, and interpret information from a variety of sources.
description_plain: Your child knows how to find, organize, and understand information from many sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate data presented in social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts.
description_plain: Your child can understand and judge information shown in tables, graphs, maps, and texts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use compelling and supporting questions to investigate social scientific problems.
description_plain: Your child can ask good questions and use them to investigate social studies problems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Inquiry, Research, And Analysis
description_plain: Your child understands how to research, ask questions, and analyze information.

subject: Social Studies
description: Present an argument supported with evidence.
description_plain: Your child can present an argument and back it up with evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Express social science ideas clearly in written, spoken, and graphic forms, including tables, line graphs, bar graphs, pie charts, maps, and images.
description_plain: Your child can clearly share social studies ideas in writing, speaking, and by using charts, graphs, maps, and pictures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret primary and secondary source documents for point of view, context, bias, and frame of reference or perspective.
description_plain: Your child can understand and explain the point of view, context, and possible bias in different types of historical documents.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use appropriate strategies to read and interpret basic social science tables, graphs, graphics, maps, and texts.
description_plain: Your child can use the right strategies to read and understand tables, graphs, maps, and texts in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Reading And Communication-Read And Communicate Effectively
description_plain: Your child can read and communicate social studies information clearly and effectively.

subject: Social Studies
description: assess the financial risk of lost income, assets, health, or identity, and determine if a person should accept the risk exposure, reduce risk, or transfer the risk to others by paying a fee now to avoid the possibility of a larger loss later.
description_plain: Your child can figure out the risks of losing money, property, health, or identity, and decide whether to accept the risk, try to lower it, or pay for insurance to protect against bigger losses later.

subject: Social Studies
description: assess the financial risk of lost income, assets, health, or identity, and determine if a person should accept the risk exposure, reduce risk, or transfer the risk to others by paying a fee now to avoid the possibility of a larger loss later.
description_plain: Your child can figure out the risks of losing money, property, health, or identity, and decide whether to accept the risk, try to lower it, or pay for insurance to protect against bigger losses later.

subject: Social Studies
description: assess the financial risk of lost income, assets, health, or identity, and determine if a person should accept the risk exposure, reduce risk, or transfer the risk to others by paying a fee now to avoid the possibility of a larger loss later.
description_plain: Your child can figure out the risks of losing money, property, health, or identity, and decide whether to accept the risk, try to lower it, or pay for insurance to protect against bigger losses later.

subject: Social Studies
description: assess the financial risk of lost income, assets, health, or identity, and determine if a person should accept the risk exposure, reduce risk, or transfer the risk to others by paying a fee now to avoid the possibility of a larger loss later.
description_plain: Your child can figure out the risks of losing money, property, health, or identity, and decide whether to accept the risk, try to lower it, or pay for insurance to protect against bigger losses later.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyze the risks, expected rate of return, tax benefits, impact of inflation, role of government agencies, and importance of diversification when investing in financial assets.
description_plain: Your child can look at the risks and rewards of investing, understand tax benefits, how inflation affects investments, the role of government, and why it's important to spread out investments.

subject: Social Studies
description: analyze the risks, expected rate of return, tax benefits, impact of inflation, role of government agencies, and importance of diversification when investing in financial assets.
description_plain: Your child can look at the risks and rewards of investing, understand tax benefits, how inflation affects investments, the role of government, and why it's important to spread out investments.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Identify the core ideals of American society as reflected in the documents below, and analyze the ways that American society moved toward and/or away from its core ideals:
  • the Declaration of Independence.
  • the original United States Constitution (including the Preamble).
  • the Bill of Rights.
  • the Gettysburg Address.
  • the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Amendments.
description_plain: Your child can identify America's main values from important documents and explain how the country has moved closer to or further from those values.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Explain the causes and consequences of contemporary population changes by analyzing the:
  • population change (including birth rate, death rate, life expectancy, growth rate, doubling time, aging population, changes in science and technology).
  • distributions of population (including relative changes in urban-rural populations, gender, age, patterns of migration, and population density).
  • relationship of the population changes to global interactions, and their impact on different regions of the world.
description_plain: Your child can explain why populations change (like birth and death rates), how people are spread out in different places, and how these changes affect the world.
